EDCS-09200748.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Iulio Martino Lucius Iulius Martius et Lucius Iulius Marcellinus patri piissimo\n
EDCS-09200748.translation: To the Divine Shades. To Lucius Julius Martinus. Lucius Julius Martius and Lucius Julius Marcellinus [dedicate this] to their most devoted father.
EDCS-09200748.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to Lucius Julius Martinus by his sons, likely from the late 1st or early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09200857.text_edition: \n Comedovis Augustis Marcus Helvius Severi filius Iuventius ex voto\n
EDCS-09200857.translation: To Comedovis Augustus. Marcus Helvius Iuventius, son of Severus, fulfilling his vow.
EDCS-09200857.summary: This is a votive dedication to the local deity Comedovis Augustus, made by Marcus Helvius Iuventius.

EDCS-09201014.translation: Born for the good of the state.
EDCS-09201014.summary: This is a concise inscription praising an individual, likely a child, for their auspicious birth for the benefit of the community.

EDCS-09201039.translation: To the Unconquered God, Genius of the place. Firmidius Severinus, soldier of the Eighth Legion Augusta Pia Fidelis Constantis Commodianae, with 26 years of service, erects this altar, having vowed it for his own safety. He willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow. Erected when Mucianus and Fabianus were consuls.
EDCS-09201039.summary: This is a votive altar dedicated to the Unconquered God and the Genius of the place by a soldier of the Eighth Legion, offering prayers for his own safety, dated to the consulship of Mucianus and Fabianus (likely 181 CE).

EDCS-09201054.translation: Marcus Carantius Macrinus, centurion of the first urban cohort, became a soldier in the same cohort when Domitian II was consul. He served as an adjutant to Tettienus Serenus, imperial legate, when Vespasian was consul [for the] 9th time. He served as a cornicularius to Cornelius Gallicanus, imperial legate with equestrian rank. He served as an evocatus Augusti when Domitian was consul [for the] 14th time. He became centurion when the Emperor Nerva was consul. He ordered this to be set up in his will.
EDCS-09201054.summary: This is a military career inscription detailing the promotions and appointments of Marcus Carantius Macrinus, from soldier to centurion, serving under various legates and emperors, and ordered to be set up in his will, spanning from the late 1st century to the early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09201171.text_edition: \n Marcus Rutilius Firminus nomine Cai Rutili Frontini filii sui de suo dedit Mercurio ex voto Marcus Rutilius Firminus nomine Cai Rutili Frontini filii sui de suo dedit\n
EDCS-09201171.translation: Marcus Rutilius Firminus, on behalf of his son Caius Rutilius Frontinus, dedicated this at his own expense to Mercury, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-09201171.summary: A dedication to Mercury by Marcus Rutilius Firminus for his son, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09201290.text_edition: \n s Mariae onis filiae uxsori! et sibi f(ecit)\n
EDCS-09201290.translation: Maria, daughter of Onis, and her wife made this for themselves.
EDCS-09201290.summary: A dedication by Maria for herself and her wife, likely a tombstone from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09201469.text_edition: \n Cnaeo Excingilli filio Solirigi uxsori filii parentibus fecerunt\n
EDCS-09201469.translation: His wife and children made this for Gnaeus Excingillus, son of Solirigus.
EDCS-09201469.summary: A funerary monument erected by the family for Gnaeus Excingillus, son of Solirigus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09201635.text_edition: \n Tito Iulio Sexti filio Voltinia Maximo Manliano Broccho Serviliano Aulo Quadronio Vero Lucio Servilio Vatiae Cassio Camarti legato Augusti legionis IIII Flaviae legato Augusti legionis I adiutricis legato Augusti iuridico Hispaniae citerioris Tarraconensis praetori aedili curuli quaestori provinciae Hispaniae ulterioris Baeticae donis donato in bello Dacico coronis murali et vallari hasta pura vexillo tribuno militum legionis V Macedonicae seviro equitum Romanorum turmae I Xviro stlitibus iudicandis Calagurritani ex Hispania Citeriore patrono\n
EDCS-09201635.translation: To Titus Julius Maximus Manlianus Brocchus Servilianus, son of Titus Julius Sextus, of the Voltinian tribe, from Calagurris in Lower Spain, who was honored by gifts in the Dacian War with a mural crown and a vallary crown, and with a pure spear and vexillum; who was military tribune of the IV Flavia Legion, legate of the I Adiutrix Legion, legate of the governor in Hispania Citerior, praetor, curule aedile, quaestor of the province of Baetica in Further Spain, and patron; who was also decorated with a pure spear and vexillum, tribune of soldiers of the V Macedonica Legion, a seviri of Roman knights of the First Cavalry Turma, and served as XVIvir for judging lawsuits; he was a patron.
EDCS-09201635.summary: An extensive funerary epitaph for Titus Julius Maximus Manlianus Brocchus Servilianus, detailing his extensive military and administrative career, likely from the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09201648.text_edition: \n Decreto decurionum memoriae Sexti Iulii Sexti filii Voltinia Maximi flaminis Romae et divi Augusti item Drusi et Germanici Caesarum tribuni militum praefecti fabrum III IIIIviri iure dicundo Nemausenses\n
EDCS-09201648.translation: By decree of the town council, in memory of Sextus Julius Maximus, flamen of Rome and the deified Augustus, and also of Drusus and Germanicus Caesar, military tribune, prefect of the fabrica, and aedile, the people of Nemausus honored him.
EDCS-09201648.summary: A monument honoring Sextus Julius Maximus, who held several important civic and religious offices in Nemausus, erected by decree of the local council, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09201701.translation: The sacred synod of Naples, in its quinquennial contest, decreed to Titus Julius Dolabella, son of Titus Julius, of the Voltinian tribe, a quattuorvir of the treasury, a pontiff, and prefect of the vigiles and armor. He was honored at Hadrian's [Games?]. The sacred synod of the guild of centonarii decreed... [Greek text follows, confirming the honor for Dolabella, praising his lineage, character, and achievements, and mentioning his patron and the city of Nemausus].
EDCS-09201701.summary: A decree by a Neapolitan guild honoring Titus Julius Dolabella for his achievements, including possibly in athletic or artistic contests, with extensive Greek text detailing his merits and connections to Nemausus, likely from the Hadrianic period.

EDCS-09201997.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cocusiae Masuciae Cocusia Eutychis liberta piissima\n
EDCS-09201997.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Cocusia Masucia, freedwoman of Cocusia Eutychis, [dedicated this] with the utmost piety.
EDCS-09201997.summary: A funerary dedication for Cocusia Masucia, a freedwoman, by her former owner, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09202550.translation: Born for the good of the state.
EDCS-09202550.summary: An epitaph possibly for a child or young person, highlighting their potential or presumed contribution to public life, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09202575.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aviulli Macrini filii Cornelia Candida filio \n
EDCS-09202575.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Cornelia Candida, [dedicated this] for her son Aviullus Macrinus.
EDCS-09202575.summary: A funerary dedication for Aviullus Macrinus by his mother Cornelia Candida, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09300371.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Naeviae Aprhodisiae sic vixit annisa amicae optimae\n
EDCS-09300371.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. To Naevia Aphrodisia, his most excellent friend, who lived [this many] years.
EDCS-09300371.summary: A funerary dedication for Naevia Aphrodisia, described as a 'most excellent friend', likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09300372.text_edition: \n Paula sive Eustathia qui vixit fidelis annum unum et mensis septem et dies XXII\n
EDCS-09300372.translation: Paula or Eustathia, who lived faithfully for one year, seven months, and twenty-two days.
EDCS-09300372.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Paula or Eustathia, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-09301385.translation: Mediocratus Verus and Blaesidia (?) Extricata gave this from their own funds.
EDCS-09301385.summary: This is a dedication, likely of a monument or offering, by Mediocratus Verus and Blaesidia Extricata, with an indication that they funded it themselves, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-09301386.translation: Lucius Coelius Rufus, his wife Iulia Severa, and his son Lucius Coelius Mangius willingly and deservedly fulfilled their vow to Divannus and the Martes.
EDCS-09301386.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by Lucius Coelius Rufus, his wife Iulia Severa, and son Lucius Coelius Mangius to the deities Divannus and the Martes, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-09301395.translation: The people of Baeterras dedicated this to Marcus Julius Philippus, most noble Caesar, prince of youth.
EDCS-09301395.summary: This is a dedication by the inhabitants of Baeterras to Marcus Julius Philippus, likely a young imperial prince, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-09301406.translation: To Quintus Caecilius Homullus, son of Quintus, of the Pupinia tribe, quaestor, aedile, and twice duovir of the colony of Victrix Iulia Baeterra, […
EDCS-09301406.summary: This is a dedication to Quintus Caecilius Homullus, a prominent local official in the colony of Baeterras, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-09301411.translation: To Flavia Quincta, freedwoman of the emperor. A plot was granted by decree of the decurions, by her freedmen.
EDCS-09301411.summary: This inscription records the granting of a burial plot by freedmen to Flavia Quincta, a freedwoman of the emperor, by order of the local council, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-09301426.translation: Quintus Ancharius Primus, freedman of Mundus, for himself and for his sister Ancharia Mundana, freedwoman of Quintus; for Quintus Ancharius Primulus, freedman of Quintus; and for Ancharia Prisca, freedwoman of Quintus.
EDCS-09301426.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the allocation of burial plots by Quintus Ancharius Primus for himself, his sister, and other relatives, all of whom were freedmen or freedwomen, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-09301433.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. ...lius Coelius Quintio ... his daughter Vera made this.
EDCS-09301433.summary: This is a funerary epitaph, likely set up by Vera for her father Coelius Quintio, dedicated to the underworld deities, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-09301445.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Iulia Eutychia, a most distinguished woman. She [made this] for herself.
EDCS-09301445.summary: This is a self-funded funerary epitaph for Iulia Eutychia, a distinguished woman, dedicated to the underworld deities, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-09301448.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Maecius Alexander made this for his dearest wife, Maecia Vera, and for himself while alive.
EDCS-09301448.summary: A funerary monument set up by a husband for his wife and himself, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09301482.translation: Sacred to Juno. For Appeae Cila, freedwoman of Sextus.
EDCS-09301482.summary: A religious dedication to Juno, likely a private offering, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09301487.translation: For the Mother of the Gods. The taurobolium, which Axia, daughter of Caius, Paulina, priestess, performed, dedicated by Quintus Paquius Chrestus.
EDCS-09301487.summary: A record of a taurobolium ritual performed for the Magna Mater, involving a priestess and a dedicant, possibly from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09301490.translation: For the Mother of the Gods. Liguria Thymele received the taurobolium by decree and set up the altar (?) for the rites.
EDCS-09301490.summary: A dedication to the Mother of the Gods recording the reception of a taurobolium and the erection of an altar, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09301497.translation: For Apollo Augustus and the Divine Spirits of the Emperors. Sacred. Iulia Natalis arranged for the addition of a tetrastyle and all the bronze fittings at her own expense and dedicated them herself. The site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-09301497.summary: A public works dedication by Iulia Natalis for Apollo and the Imperial numina, including architectural additions, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09301506.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus, son of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, consul for the second time. Quintus S...ilius S...anus, member of the IIII viri Augustales, ordered this to be made and set up from his modest means. At the urn of power (?).
EDCS-09301506.summary: A dedication to Emperor Trajan by a local magistrate (IIII vir Augustalis), likely from the early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09301509.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, son of the deified Antoninus Pius, grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the fourth time, imperator for the second time, consul for the second time, proconsul. The decumani of Narbonne.
EDCS-09301509.summary: An official dedication to Emperor Lucius Verus, likely from the 160s CE, by the public officials (decumani) of Narbonne.

EDCS-09301510.translation: For Iulia Domna Augusta, wife of Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus, father of the fatherland, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the fourth time, imperator for the eighth time, consul for the second time, and for Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Caesar, mother of the Emperor and also of the camps. The decumani of Narbonne.
EDCS-09301510.summary: A dedication to Julia Domna and the imperial family by the decumani of Narbonne, likely from the period 202-211 CE.

EDCS-09301512.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Pius Felix, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, father of the fatherland. The people of Narbonne.
EDCS-09301512.summary: A public dedication to Emperor Marcus Aurelius, likely from the late 170s or early 180s CE, by the citizens of Narbonne.

EDCS-09301519.translation: Lucius Aemilius Moschus, IIII vir Augustalis, dedicated this for his most excellent patron, Lucius Aemilius Arcanus, son of Lucius, of the Papiria tribe, tribune of the soldiers of the XI Gemina legion and tribune of the soldiers of the I Minervia legion, also tribune of the soldiers of the II Augusta legion, who had held all the magistracies in his own colony, was admitted into the most distinguished order by the Emperor Caesar Hadrian Augustus, served as a IIII vir of Roman knights, pontifex, urban quaestor, tribune of the plebs, and praetor designate. After his death, Lucius Aemilius Moschus, IIII vir Augustalis, contributed 4,000 sesterces from the treasury of the sevirate for the place and protection of the statue. The site was granted by decree of the IIII viri, and with the sportula.
EDCS-09301519.summary: A funerary monument and statue base erected by a freedman patron (Lucius Aemilius Moschus) for his deceased patron (Lucius Aemilius Arcanus), a distinguished local official, likely from the Hadrianic period.

EDCS-09301528.translation: To Gallo, former aedile and curator of grain(?), to Caesar(?), prefect of the engineers, and to his brother Senicio, aedile and curator of grain(?), to his sister Messia, daughter of Marcus, Quartae, of the Voltinian tribe.
EDCS-09301528.summary: This inscription, likely a funerary monument, lists several individuals holding various magistracies and family relationships.

EDCS-09301529.translation: To Marcus Maecius Oceanus, son of Marcus, freedman of Numisia. To Gaius Maecius Ingenuus, equestrian of the X Gemina legion. To Quintus Pompeius Philotaerus, deceased. To Valeria Severa, daughter of Lucius, alive.
EDCS-09301529.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring multiple individuals, including a soldier and freedmen, with some deceased and some alive.

EDCS-09301530.translation: Marcus Marcius Rufus, son of [3], standard-bearer of the IIII Macedonica legion, for his share, for himself and his family, and for his fellow soldier Antistius [3], son of [3], of the Galerian tribe, Paternus, 15 feet in front and 15 feet in depth.
EDCS-09301530.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a standard-bearer of the IIII Macedonica legion, dedicating a plot of land for himself, his family, and a fellow soldier.

EDCS-09301532.translation: To Aulus Rubrius Priscus, son of [3], of the Pollian tribe, a veteran.
EDCS-09301532.summary: This is a dedication to Aulus Rubrius Priscus, a veteran of the Pollian tribe.

EDCS-09301533.translation: To Quintus Septimius [3] and the standard-bearer of the IIII Macedonica legion, from Forum Julii, and to Hortensia Montana, wife.
EDCS-09301533.summary: This inscription dedicates a plot of land or monument to Quintus Septimius and a standard-bearer of the IIII Macedonica legion from Forum Julii, and to his wife Hortensia Montana.

EDCS-09301535.translation: While alive, Valerius Titullus, son of Titus, of the Fabia tribe, from Gabales, a veteran.
EDCS-09301535.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Valerius Titullus, a veteran from Gabales, set up by himself during his lifetime.

EDCS-09301536.text_edition: \n duomvir quinquennalis duomvir iterum praefectus pro duoviro augur tribunus militum primipilus praefectus navium ex conlegio Honoris et Virtutis arbitratu Zenonis liberti\n
EDCS-09301536.translation: Duovir for five years, duovir for a second term, prefect, acting duovir, augur, military tribune, primipilus, prefect of the ships, from the college of Honor and Virtue, by the judgment of Zeno, freedman.
EDCS-09301536.summary: This inscription lists the extensive career and honors of an individual, likely a magistrate, and mentions a college and a freedman named Zeno.

EDCS-09301540.translation: While alive, to Lucius Aemilius Philomusus, a seviro Augustale.
EDCS-09301540.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Aemilius Philomusus, a member of the seviri Augustales, set up by himself during his lifetime.

EDCS-09301544.translation: To Gaius Allius, son of Lucius [3], husband of N[3], [set up] by his wife [3] in Narbo Martius [3].
EDCS-09301544.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Allius, dedicated by his wife and likely from Narbo Martius.

EDCS-09301546.translation: Deceased. To Gaius Annius Proculus, son of Gaius [3], a duovir Augustalis, 15 feet in the field.
EDCS-09301546.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Annius Proculus, a duovir Augustalis, recording a plot of land.

EDCS-09301549.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely names a sevirus Augustalis, a prominent freedman official in Roman towns, and his patron or a co-official, with the date and specific context lost.

EDCS-09301554.translation: To Communi's freedman Cinnamus, (a) sevirus Augustalis of the colony Narbo Martius. To Priscia his wife, Concessa, Priscia, Priscus. This monument will not pass to his heir, nor to the heir of his heir.
EDCS-09301554.summary: This is a funerary epitaph in Narbo Martius, setting up a tomb for Communi's freedman Cinnamus, his wife Priscia, and others, with a stipulation that the monument is not inheritable.

EDCS-09301555.translation: To Sextus Fadius Papirius Secundus Musa, who held all offices in the colony Narbo. Curator of the new temple of Augustus in Narbo Martius. The sub-aediani (artisans working under the aedes) of Narbonenses, to their patron, for his merits. The place was granted by decree of the decurions. The exemplar of an epistula of Sextus Fadius Papirius Secundi Musae in the words written below. Fadius Secundus to the college of Narbonensian artisans, greetings. Although it is difficult to repay your many and constant merits towards me, I know that it will be most pleasing to your affection, provided that I donate sixteen million sesterces among children and the most distinguished grandson Iucundus on the Kalends of May, the first day of my birthday, to your chest, and on that day I will pay the interest of the entire year at eight asses per person, so that my gift may be even more welcome. Henceforth I ask of your devotion that you divide the interest of this sum on that day in the most honorable dress among those present and feasting, forever, and that this sum be not converted to any other use, since I both provide for this with this letter and will provide for it in my subsequent tablets, so that if the condition written above is changed or omitted, that money may belong to [---] or if they delay in demanding the money, to the treasury of the greatest prince. If you approve of this my will, and your assent, I most earnestly ask that you inscribe it on bronze tablets and display it before the temple of Augustus (?) and write it on the left side of the statue which you have erected for me, so that there may be more certain proof of future observance in my desire. Then it was noted by the hand of Fadius Secundus. I have written this by mandate on the Kalends of October, when Orfitus and Priscus were consuls. You will keep the letter as a completed instrument. I wish you to be well, my best and dearest masters. For the purpose of the perpetual preservation and celebration of this liberality, the subaediani artisans of Narbo have decreed that the exemplar, compared with the bronze tablet, be placed in a most frequented place before the temple.
EDCS-09301555.summary: This lengthy inscription from Narbo Martius details a substantial endowment by Sextus Fadius Secundus Musa, a former official, to the college of local artisans, providing for an annual feast and distribution of interest, with instructions for its perpetual observance and inscription on public records.

EDCS-09301557.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-09301557.translation: To Sex(tus) Fadius Secundus Musa, of the Papiria tribe, who has fulfilled all offices in the colony of Narbo, perhaps first curator of the new Temple of Augustus in Narbo Martius, the subaedial craftsmen of Narbo dedicated this space to their patron by decree of the decurions for his merits. Exemplary letter of Sex(tus) Fadius Secundus Musa to the guild of craftsmen of Narbo: "Fadius Secundus to the guild of craftsmen of Narbo, greetings. Although it is difficult to repay your many and constant services to me, I know what will be most welcome to your affection: I will deposit 16,000 sesterces in your treasury on the Kalends of May, my birthday, and on that day I will pay the interest for the entire year at eight asses, so that my gift may be even more pleasing. Furthermore, I ask of your piety that you divide the interest of this sum annually on that day, in the most honorable attire among those present and feasting, and that this sum be converted to no other use, since I am providing for this in this letter and will provide for it in my will, so that if the condition written above is changed or omitted, that money shall pertain to the treasury of the most excellent prince, or if they delay in requesting the money, to the treasury of the most excellent prince. If you approve of this my will and your consent, I most urgently request that you inscribe it on bronze tablets and display it before the Temple of Augustus and write it on the left side of the base of the statue which you have erected for me, so that there may be proof of future observance in my desire. Then it was noted by the hand of Fadius Secundus. Written by mandate, I have written this on the Kalends of October, with Orfitus and Priscus as consuls. You will keep the letter as a complete instrument. I wish you to be well, my best and dearest masters. For the sake of the perpetual preservation and celebration of this generosity, the subaedial craftsmen of Narbo have resolved that a copy, compared with the bronze tablet, should be placed before the temple in a very public place."
EDCS-09301557.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription followed by a letter from a patron to the guild of craftsmen of Narbo, detailing a bequest of 16,000 sesterces for an annual feast and specifying the inscription of his will on bronze tablets.

EDCS-09301558.translation: Fannia Celsina, freedwoman of Titus, for herself and for Gaius Trebonius Abundus, her husband, a sevirus Augustalis of Narbo Martius.
EDCS-09301558.summary: This is a funerary epitaph from Narbo Martius for Fannia Celsina and her husband Gaius Trebonius Abundus, who was a sevirus Augustalis.

EDCS-09301559.translation: To Quintus Fullonius Tolosanus, a sevirus Augustalis. By Caecilia Tertuliana, daughter of Lucius, his wife.
EDCS-09301559.summary: This is a funerary epitaph from Narbo Martius for Quintus Fullonius Tolosanus, a sevirus Augustalis, dedicated by his wife Caecilia Tertuliana.

EDCS-09301562.translation: To the divine spirits. To Tiberius Iunius Eudoxus, shipowner of the sea, of the colony Narbo Martius. Tiberius Iunius Fadianus, a sevirus Augustalis of the colony Narbo Martius, and contractor of the ironworks on the right bank, for his most devoted brother.
EDCS-09301562.summary: This is a funerary epitaph from Narbo Martius for Tiberius Iunius Eudoxus, a maritime shipowner, dedicated by his brother Tiberius Iunius Fadianus, who was also a sevirus Augustalis and a contractor for ironworks.

EDCS-09301567.translation: [---] to Modestus, governor acting as duovir, while alive. To Modesta, his wife.
EDCS-09301567.summary: This inscription, though fragmentary, appears to honor a governor acting as a duovir and his wife, Modesta, in a Roman town, likely Narbo Martius given the context of other inscriptions.

EDCS-09301571.translation: In the consulship of Sextus Papinius, Quintus Plautius, Gaius Vettius Rufus, Marcus Porcius Cato.
EDCS-09301571.summary: This inscription records the names of six consuls, likely marking a specific year or event, though the location and full context are lost.

EDCS-09301572.translation: To Gaius Pellius Agathodorus, Gaius Pellius Anthos, Gaius Pellius Silvanus, one of the six priests of the Augustales.
EDCS-09301572.summary: This inscription from Narbo Martius lists three individuals, with Gaius Pellius Silvanus identified as a sevirus Augustalis, likely serving as a dedication or funerary monument.

EDCS-09301580.translation: To Quintus Rammius Fronton, son of freedman Quintus, freedman of Quintus, *sevir* Augustalis, 15 feet in the field.
EDCS-09301580.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Rammius Fronton, noting his status as a freedman and *sevir* Augustalis.

EDCS-09301581.translation: To Rammia Stasia, freedwoman of Cn. Cn., his wife; to Publius Vinicius, son of Marcus, *aedile*, *praefect* for the *duumvirate*.
EDCS-09301581.summary: This inscription honors Rammia Stasia and Publius Vinicius, detailing their civic and administrative roles.

EDCS-09301583.translation: To Lucius Scanianius Atticus, patron, *sevir* Augustalis.
EDCS-09301583.summary: This is a dedication to Lucius Scanianius Atticus, recognizing him as a patron and *sevir* Augustalis.

EDCS-09301585.translation: To Gaius Septimienus Nedymus, freedman of Sapula, freedman of Gaius, *sevir* Augustalis.
EDCS-09301585.summary: This inscription records Gaius Septimienus Nedymus, a freedman and *sevir* Augustalis.

EDCS-09301586.translation: Quintus Terentius Serenus, son of freedman Quintus, freedman of Quintus, *sevir* Augustalis of the colony Julia Patera Claudia Narbonensis Martius, alive, [erected this] to his most excellent and most devoted patron and to Octavia Valentina and her husband Titus Pompeius Firmus, and to their children Firmus and Pompeia Firmula, and to all their freedmen and freedwomen and their children and freedmen, and to himself and his family.
EDCS-09301586.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Quintus Terentius Serenus for his patron, family members, and himself, detailing his status as a *sevir* Augustalis in Narbonne.

EDCS-09301587.summary: This is a fragmented inscription mentioning Veiatius as an *aedile*.

EDCS-09301588.translation: To Lucius Vercio Priscus, *sevir* Augustalis of the colony Julia Patera Claudia Narbonensis Martius, and to the Baths of Sextis. Marcus Aurunceius, son of Marcus [---], to his most excellent friend.
EDCS-09301588.summary: This inscription dedicates a monument to Lucius Vercio Priscus, a *sevir* Augustalis, and also mentions the Baths of Sextis, with a dedication to a friend.

EDCS-09301589.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Publius Umidius Zoilus, *sevir* Augustalis of the corporate body in Narbonne. Pompeia Myrine to her most devoted husband.
EDCS-09301589.summary: This is a funerary inscription from Narbonne, dedicated by Pompeia Myrine to her husband, Publius Umidius Zoilus, a *sevir* Augustalis.

EDCS-09301611.translation: Cornelius Paetus, son of Publius, *lictore viator* of the colony Julia Patera Narbonensis Martius, 15 feet.
EDCS-09301611.summary: This inscription records Cornelius Paetus, identified as a *lictore viator* in Narbonne.

EDCS-09301612.translation: Gaius Manlius Rufus Umber, son of Gaius, of the Papirian tribe, made [this] for himself and his family from the *decuria* of *lictores viatores* which is in the colony Julia Patera Narbonensis Martius, with altars, to Gaius Cassius Clement, and to [---]sidia Restituta, his companion, and to Gaius Cassius Exoratus, his son, and to Gaius Cassius Viator, his freedman.
EDCS-09301612.summary: This is a funerary monument created by Gaius Manlius Rufus Umber for his family and associates, noting his membership in the *decuria* of *lictores viatores* in Narbonne.

EDCS-09301619.translation: Philomusus, freedman of Caius, a flask-maker. Frugi is buried here.
EDCS-09301619.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Philomusus, a freedman and flask-maker, and Frugi, likely his associate or family member, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-09301620.translation: In his lifetime, Numerius Consius Dionysius, a ring-maker, for himself and for his deceased patron Numerius Consius Eronus. 15 feet in front.
EDCS-09301620.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Numerius Consius Dionysius, a freedman and ring-maker, for himself and his deceased patron, dating to the Imperial period and specifying the plot size.

EDCS-09301621.translation: Quintus Fuficius Eleuter, freedman of Quintus, son of Titus, and Philotis, freedwoman of [---], silver-workers, made from their own resources.
EDCS-09301621.summary: This is a dedication by two silver-workers, Quintus Fuficius Eleuter and Philotis, who may have been freedpersons, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-09301623.translation: to the silver-worker; to Turpa, freedwoman of Caius, daughter of [---].
EDCS-09301623.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription that likely records a dedication to a silver-worker and a freedwoman, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-09301624.translation: May they live: Herophilus the [---]-maker, for himself and for Lendia Riccia, and for his son, and for the freedman Cassius Popilius. This monument will not pass to his heir, nor to his freedmen, nor to his descendants. 15 feet in every direction.
EDCS-09301624.summary: This is a funerary inscription intended to ensure the plot is not inherited by heirs, freedmen, or descendants, specifying its dimensions, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-09301628.summary: This is a brief inscription identifying Lucius Cornelius Amandus as a goldsmith, likely from a tombstone or dedication, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-09301629.translation: In his lifetime, Cornelius Castor, freedman of Caius, for himself and for the deceased Marcus Fonteius Acanthus, goldsmith, and for his companion Gluco, and for Chloe Valeria.
EDCS-09301629.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Cornelius Castor, a freedman and goldsmith, for himself and several others including his companion, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-09301630.translation: May they live: Lucius Oppius Quietus, a bridle-maker, and Malia Cara, his companion for [3] years, live without quarreling.
EDCS-09301630.summary: This is an inscription expressing a wish for the peaceful coexistence of Lucius Oppius Quietus, a bridle-maker, and his companion Malia Cara, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-09301631.translation: May they live: Lucius Cervius Zetus, freedman of Lucius, a club-bearer and material supplier, for himself and for Notae, freedwoman. Iucunda, a freedwoman of Frugi, made it. [3] feet into the land.
EDCS-09301631.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Iucunda, a freedwoman, for Lucius Cervius Zetus, a freedman and his associates, specifying the plot size, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-09301633.translation: Gaius Julius Servatus, son of Gaius, and Gaius Salius Primus, son of Gaius, wine-merchants, erected this at their own expense.
EDCS-09301633.summary: This is a dedication by two wine-merchants, Gaius Julius Servatus and Gaius Salius Primus, to an unspecified deity or for a public purpose, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-09301634.translation: To Quintus Catins Hermes, freedman of Quintus, a kitchen worker. [Measurements: in the field 30 feet, in the front 20 feet.]
EDCS-09301634.summary: This inscription seems to record land boundaries or a property marker for Quintus Catins Hermes, a freedman employed in the kitchen.

EDCS-09301636.translation: Lives Marcus Paquius Dama, freedman of Marcus, a rope maker. [Measurement: 15 feet in the field.]
EDCS-09301636.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Paquius Dama, a freedman and rope maker, indicating his place of burial or a memorial plot.

EDCS-09301638.summary: This inscription identifies Gaius Cornelius Philonicus as a silversmith.

EDCS-09301639.translation: Quintus Baebius Tertius, freedman of Quintus, a sieve maker, rests here on his own property.
EDCS-09301639.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Quintus Baebius Tertius, a freedman and sieve maker, who is buried on his own land.

EDCS-09301640.translation: Lives Mustius Surito, a sieve maker. [Incomplete inscription]
EDCS-09301640.summary: This is an incomplete funerary inscription for Mustius Surito, identified as a sieve maker.

EDCS-09301643.text_edition: \n Vivit Publius Usulenus Hilarae libertus Anoptes gypsarius |(obitus) et Communi filio vivae Usulenae Publi libertae Hilarae uxsori sic in fronte pedes XII\n
EDCS-09301643.translation: Lives Publius Usulenus Anoptes, freedman of Hilara, a plasterer, and his daughter Communi, and his living wife Usulena, freedwoman of Publius Hilara. [Measurement: 12 feet in the front.]
EDCS-09301643.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Publius Usulenus Anoptes, a freedman and plasterer, his daughter Communi, and his wife Usulena, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09301645.translation: Gaius Nigidius Primus, a wool dealer, for himself and Probus, Meros (?), and his freedmen (?).
EDCS-09301645.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or a funerary marker for Gaius Nigidius Primus, a wool dealer, and potentially other individuals including freedmen.

EDCS-09301646.translation: Lives Lucius Sabidius Capito, freedman of Lucius, a wool dealer.
EDCS-09301646.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Sabidius Capito, a freedman and wool dealer.

EDCS-09301647.translation: Lucius Nerfinius Primus, freedman of Potitus, a pork butcher, for himself and [...]
EDCS-09301647.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication for Lucius Nerfinius Primus, a freedman and pork butcher, for himself and possibly others.

EDCS-09301648.translation: V[---]ius Varus, freedman of Anterotis, a linen weaver, for himself and for his wife Betutia Privata, freedwoman of Marcus. [Measurement: 15 feet in the front.]
EDCS-09301648.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial place of Varus, a freedman and linen weaver, and his wife Betutia Privata, with specified front dimensions.

EDCS-09301651.translation: Heraclides, freedman of Xanth(e)rmis, doctor, here buried. Iacchus Lepotinis made this for his patron Lepos, aged 13, from his own funds.
EDCS-09301651.summary: A tombstone commemorating Heraclides, a doctor, and his patron Lepos, who died at age 13, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09301653.translation: Lucius Pomponius Diocles, freedman of Lucius, doctor, while alive, for himself and for his wife Atilia Quarta.
EDCS-09301653.summary: A funerary monument erected by Lucius Pomponius Diocles, a doctor, for himself and his wife, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09301655.translation: Gaius Vettienus, son of Titus, of the Pollia tribe, money changer, is buried here.
EDCS-09301655.summary: A tombstone for Gaius Vettienus, a money changer, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09301656.translation: Gaius Offellius Zetus, freedman of Gaius, honest merchant.
EDCS-09301656.summary: An epitaph for Gaius Offellius Zetus, a merchant and freedman, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09301657.translation: Lucius Gaienina Masclus, ship owner, lives on for himself and his family, 15 feet in front.
EDCS-09301657.summary: A funerary monument indicating a burial plot of 15 feet in front, for Lucius Gaienina Masclus, a ship owner, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09301658.translation: Gaius Valerius Gemellus of Forum Iulii, ship owner, lives on for himself and his wife Iulia Quina, daughter of Marcus, 15 feet in every direction.
EDCS-09301658.summary: A tombstone for Gaius Valerius Gemellus, a ship owner from Forum Iulii, and his wife Iulia Quina, specifying a burial plot of 15 feet in all directions, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09301659.translation: For Decimus Uleius Auctus, freedman of Decimus, ship owner, and for his wife Lucia Clara, now deceased.
EDCS-09301659.summary: A tombstone for Decimus Uleius Auctus, a ship owner, and his wife Lucia Clara, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09301660.translation: [...]enon the businessman and his wife, Sextus Avio [...] lives on by the arbitration of Communis [...].
EDCS-09301660.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription mentioning a businessman and his wife, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09301661.translation: Lucius Baebius Lepidus, money changer, lives on.
EDCS-09301661.summary: An epitaph for Lucius Baebius Lepidus, a money changer, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09301663.translation: Publius Granius Communis, freedman of Publius, olive oil dealer, lives on for himself and for Grania Flora, freedwoman of Publius.
EDCS-09301663.summary: A tombstone for Publius Granius Communis, an olive oil dealer, and his freedwoman Grania Flora, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09301664.translation: Anicia Hilara, daughter of Titus, made this for herself and for her freedman, the purse-maker Sextus Vibius Pamphilus.
EDCS-09301664.summary: This funerary inscription records a woman, Anicia Hilara, dedicating a monument to herself and her freedman, Sextus Vibius Pamphilus, who was a purse-maker.

EDCS-09301665.translation: Lives Publius Gallonius Capito, freedman of Publius, a pillar-maker, and also the freedwoman Chyteris. Gallonia Clienta, freedwoman of Publius, lived; Publius Gallonius Cliens, freedman of Capito, lives; this monument will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-09301665.summary: This funerary inscription records the living status of several individuals, including Publius Gallonius Capito, a pillar-maker, and indicates that the monument is not inheritable.

EDCS-09301666.translation: For Lucius Aponius Eros, freedman of Celatus, a candidate baker. Venusta, his partner, and Ospeus Ducieus...
EDCS-09301666.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication for Lucius Aponius Eros, freedman of Celatus, who was a candidate baker, with contributions from his partner Venusta and Ospeus Ducieus.

EDCS-09301667.translation: Lives Lucius Decumius Felix, freedman of Lucius, from Rome, a baker, for himself and for the deceased Lucius Decumius Hilarus, patron. Also to the seven altars.
EDCS-09301667.summary: This funerary inscription records Lucius Decumius Felix, a baker from Rome, dedicating the monument to himself and his patron Lucius Decumius Hilarus, also mentioning seven altars.

EDCS-09301668.translation: Lives Voltilia Secunda, freedwoman of the woman, for herself and for her husband Gnaeus Domitius Auctus, a painter.
EDCS-09301668.summary: This funerary inscription records Voltilia Secunda dedicating a monument to herself and her husband Gnaeus Domitius Auctus, a painter.

EDCS-09301670.translation: Lives Publius Albius [---]cinus, freedman of the woman, a provisioner, salaried(?), for himself and for the deceased Plania Tertia, daughter of Marcus, and for the deceased Plania Secunda, daughter of Marcus. In the field, 15 feet.
EDCS-09301670.summary: This funerary inscription records Publius Albius [---]cinus, a provisioner, dedicating a monument to himself and two women, Plania Tertia and Plania Secunda, and specifies a plot size of 15 feet.

EDCS-09301671.translation: To Aulus Sempronius Laetus, freedman of Gallaecus, a dyer, and to Sempronia Modesta, his wife.
EDCS-09301671.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Aulus Sempronius Laetus, a freedman dyer, and his wife Sempronia Modesta.

EDCS-09301672.translation: Lives Aulus Sempronius Laetus, freedman of Gallaecus, a dyer, for himself and Sempronia Modesta.
EDCS-09301672.summary: This funerary inscription records Aulus Sempronius Laetus, a freedman dyer, dedicating the monument to himself and Sempronia Modesta.

EDCS-09301673.translation: Lucius Actorius Sphaerus, freedman of Lucius, a maker of nets or straps.
EDCS-09301673.summary: This inscription identifies Lucius Actorius Sphaerus, freedman of Lucius, as a maker of nets or straps (sagarius).

EDCS-09301674.summary: This inscription identifies Lucius Braetius Litorinus as a sandal-maker (solearius).

EDCS-09301675.translation: Lucius Autronius Rufus, the builder, lives; Geminia Amoena, freedwoman of Aulus, lives.
EDCS-09301675.summary: This inscription records two individuals, Lucius Autronius Rufus and Geminia Amoena, who are noted as living, with Rufus identified as a builder (constructor).

EDCS-09301676.translation: Gaius Rabirius Hilarius, the scribe, lives.
EDCS-09301676.summary: This inscription records the survival of Gaius Rabirius Hilarius, identified as a scribe (tabellarius).

EDCS-09301677.translation: Lucrio, freedman, made [this] for Papirius Rufus, the shopkeeper, and for his wife, Silvana, and for [---]raia. Lives; Antonia, freedwoman of Titus; [---]vena, freedwoman of Titus.
EDCS-09301677.summary: This inscription records the creation of a monument by Lucrio for Papirius Rufus, a shopkeeper, and his wife Silvana, also mentioning other individuals who are noted as living.

EDCS-09301678.translation: Marcus Coelius Onesimus, freedman of Marcus, for himself and for Primigenia, his freedwoman companion, and for his mother Cervia Fusca, the hairdresser.
EDCS-09301678.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Marcus Coelius Onesimus for himself, his companion Primigenia, and his mother Cervia Fusca, a hairdresser.

EDCS-09301679.translation: Titus Olitius Hermes, the hairdresser, made [this] for himself, and for his wife Olitia Amabilis, and for his son Olitius Iulianus, and for their descendants. Lives.
EDCS-09301679.summary: This is a funerary inscription made by Titus Olitius Hermes, a hairdresser, for himself, his wife, son, and descendants.

EDCS-09301680.translation: Voltilia Optata, daughter of Decimus, lives. For father and mother, Decimus Voltilius Auctus, the hairdresser, and Optata, daughter of Spurius, 15 feet in all directions.
EDCS-09301680.summary: This inscription records the survival of Voltilia Optata and the dedication of a burial plot of 15 square feet by Decimus Voltilius Auctus, a hairdresser, and Optata for their parents.

EDCS-09301681.translation: the hairdresser, human, 15 feet in all directions.
EDCS-09301681.summary: This inscription records a burial plot of 15 square feet belonging to an individual identified only as a hairdresser.

EDCS-09301682.translation: Euphorus Casius Niger [for his] freedman Nigro, the incense seller.
EDCS-09301682.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Euphorus Casius Niger to his freedman Nigro, who was an incense seller (thurarius).

EDCS-09301684.translation: Marcus Accius Primus, freedman of Marcus, the clothier, lives; for himself and for Optatus, his freedman.
EDCS-09301684.summary: This inscription records the survival of Marcus Accius Primus, a clothier, and his dedication for himself and his freedman Optatus.

EDCS-09301685.translation: Publius Licinius Licinus, freedman of Publius, the clothier, lives; for himself and his family. Sergia [---], for herself and her family.
EDCS-09301685.summary: This inscription records the survival of Publius Licinius Licinus, a clothier, and his dedication for his family, as well as a similar dedication by Sergia.

EDCS-09301712.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Aelia Restituta, sweetest soul. Bellator, freedman of the Emperor, for his most rare wife who deserved well of him. Friends, 'while we live, let us live'.
EDCS-09301712.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated by a freedman to his wife, expressing a sentiment about enjoying life.

EDCS-09301944.translation: Titus Vale[rius Ephebus], freedman of Titus, [perhaps a craftsman]? L(ucius) Vitella, daughter of Lucius. Valerius Epaphroditus.
EDCS-09301944.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription listing at least three individuals, one of whom may have been a craftsman.

EDCS-09301960.translation: Lives. Marcus Fabius Stabilio, for his father Marcus Fabius Philargurus, and for his wife Pompeia Tertullina, daughter of Lucius, and for his wet-nurse Fabia Rustica, and for his mother Fabia Salvia. In front 10 feet.
EDCS-09301960.summary: A funerary inscription that records a man dedicating a tomb to his father, wife, wet-nurse, and mother, with a measurement likely indicating the width of the plot.

EDCS-09302141.translation: Lives. For Lucius Marius Masclus, son of Lucius, a leatherworker, and for Gimia Optata, his companion [---] Marius [---] his wife.
EDCS-09302141.summary: A funerary inscription for a leatherworker and his companion and wife.

EDCS-09302144.translation: ] Martius, freedman of the colony Diocha[res]. Sabidia [---] daughter of Numerius. [---] Lucaea. Is buried here.
EDCS-09302144.summary: A funerary inscription indicating that at least three individuals, including a freedman of the colony, are buried here.

EDCS-09302189.translation: Lives. Quintus Numisius Proculus, son of Quintus, a muleteer, for himself and for Quintus Numisius Eronus, his father, and for Caelia Silvana, his mother.
EDCS-09302189.summary: A funerary inscription set up by a muleteer for himself, his father, and his mother.

EDCS-09302557.translation: Lives. Lucius Salonius Buccio, freedman of Lucius and Publius, for himself and for the deceased Lucius Salonius Hilarus, freedman of Lucius and Publius, a salt worker.
EDCS-09302557.summary: A funerary inscription by a freedman for himself and another freedman who was a salt worker.

EDCS-09302563.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus, Pious, Fortunate, Invincible Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the second time, consul, father of the country. The decumani of Narbo.
EDCS-09302563.summary: An inscription dedicating the honor to Emperor Gordian III, likely by the tax collectors ('decumani') of Narbo.

EDCS-09302574.translation: Titus Valerius Senecio, son of Caius; Publius Usulenus Phileros, freedman of Veienton; Titus Alfidius Stabilio, freedman of Titus; Marcus Usulenus Charito, freedman of Marcus. As magistrates of the pagi, from the income of the shrine, they took care to have cells built and they approved them.
EDCS-09302574.summary: An inscription by four local magistrates (magistri pagi) who oversaw the construction and approval of shrine cells using temple funds.

EDCS-09302575.translation: To Caius Cominius Bituitionus, son of Caius, of the Voltinia tribe, praetor of the colony of Carpento[rate?].
EDCS-09302575.summary: A dedication to Caius Cominius Bituitionus, identified as a praetor of the colony of Carpento[rate?].

EDCS-09302577.translation: Fortunata fulfills her vow to Jupiter willingly.
EDCS-09302577.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter by Fortunata.

EDCS-09302580.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Pompeius Polibius sic votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09302580.translation: Caius Pompeius Polibius willingly and deservedly fulfills his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-09302580.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Caius Pompeius Polibius.

EDCS-09302581.translation: Caius Pompeius Quartus willingly and with a willing heart dedicates to his Mars.
EDCS-09302581.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Mars by Caius Pompeius Quartus.

EDCS-09302603.translation: Here rests the well-remembered Doctorbodes, who lived thirty-five years.
EDCS-09302603.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Doctorbodes, who died at the age of thirty-five.

EDCS-09302605.translation: Here lies the well-remembered Eugenius, who lived fifteen years and nine months; he rested on the 10th day before the Kalends of February.
EDCS-09302605.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Eugenius, who died at the age of fifteen years and nine months.

EDCS-09302606.translation: The burial of Felicisima, who lived 45 years, servant of God; she rested on the 13th day before the Kalends of May.
EDCS-09302606.summary: This is a Christian funerary inscription for Felicisima, who died at the age of 45.

EDCS-09302607.translation: Here lies Hiraclius the newly born, who lived 21 years and [3?] months, buried [on...]
EDCS-09302607.summary: This is a fragmentary Christian funerary inscription for Hiraclius, who died at the age of 21 years and some months.

EDCS-09302608.translation: Here lies Martolus, who lived three months; he rests in peace.
EDCS-09302608.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Martolus, who died at the age of three months.

EDCS-09302611.translation: Here rests the well-remembered Rodulfus, who lived thirty-five years; he rests in the Lord's peace.
EDCS-09302611.summary: This is a Christian funerary inscription for Rodulfus, who died at the age of thirty-five.

EDCS-09302612.translation: Here rests Sedata, who lived more or less 50 years.
EDCS-09302612.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sedata, who lived approximately 50 years.

EDCS-09302653.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinti Claudi Paterni et Aemiliae Paternae Quintus Claudius Nepos pientissimis parentibus fecit\n
EDCS-09302653.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Quintus Claudius Nepos made this for his most dutiful parents, Quintus Claudius Paternus and Aemilia Patera.
EDCS-09302653.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by a son for his deceased parents.

EDCS-09302802.translation: from the [city/people of] Cabellenses, leader of the youth, son of Marcus Opellius Severus Macrinus Pius Felix Augustus.
EDCS-09302802.summary: This inscription honors a young leader, possibly related to the emperor Macrinus, from the town of Cabellenses.

EDCS-09302838.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Gratia, most holy. Gratius Gratus [dedicated this] to his most dutiful father and to Gratia Grata, daughter of Gratia Gratia.
EDCS-09302838.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Gratia, honoring her parents and her father Gratius Gratus, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-09302864.text_edition: \n Escingillus Valtaci Parcabus votum solvit libens animo\n
EDCS-09302864.translation: Escingillus paid his vow to the Parcæ willingly.
EDCS-09302864.summary: This votive inscription records an individual named Escingillus fulfilling a vow to the Parcæ (Fates).

EDCS-09302937.translation: Antonia Maria, daughter of Antonius Celeris, made this for herself while alive.
EDCS-09302937.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a woman for herself.

EDCS-09302938.translation: [...] and his brother, Campanus and Restutus [died?] in the water, and one [remains?]
EDCS-09302938.summary: This fragmented inscription appears to record a tragic drowning incident involving at least two brothers.

EDCS-09302945.translation: Volumnius (?) Aurelius Cominus, a tuba player of the 13th Legion Gemina, aged 30 years, with 13 years of service.
EDCS-09302945.summary: This funerary epitaph records the name, rank (tuba player), legion, age, and service length of a soldier.

EDCS-09302949.translation: Lucius Rennius Pamphilus, a bath attendant (?).
EDCS-09302949.summary: This is a brief epitaph identifying an individual named Lucius Rennius Pamphilus, possibly a bath attendant.

EDCS-09302950.text_edition: \n Vivus Marcus Iulius Capito Foroiuliensis copo sibi et vivae Liciniae Luci filiae Primae uxori\n
EDCS-09302950.translation: While alive, Marcus Iulius Capito of Forum Iulii, a wine seller, [made this] for himself and for his wife, the living Licinia Prima, daughter of Lucius.
EDCS-09302950.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a wine seller for himself and his wife.

EDCS-09302951.translation: Vivit. Lucius Tullius Felix, a rope maker, [dedicated this] for himself and his patron (?) and his wife Satulla.
EDCS-09302951.summary: This funerary inscription records a rope maker who set up the monument for himself, possibly his patron, and his wife.

EDCS-09302953.translation: To Lucius Silius, freedman of Lucius, named Sandro, merchant, freedman of [---] Ligur, [in] the territory, 15 feet [in front].
EDCS-09302953.summary: This is a funerary and boundary inscription, likely for a merchant named Sandro, indicating land ownership or a burial plot.

EDCS-09302954.translation: To Lucius Squeillanius Faustus, freedman of Lucius, the ship-owner, and to Flavia Venusta, freedwoman of Marcus, his wife, by the arbitration of Venusta, my wife.
EDCS-09302954.summary: This is a funerary monument set up for a ship-owner and his wife, with the inscription indicating it was arranged by the wife.

EDCS-09302955.translation: To [---]ius Fuscus [---] and to Annia Aucta, freedwoman of Titus, his wife. [---] Julius Priscus, merchant, cloth merchant, 15 feet in front.
EDCS-09302955.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a couple, likely commemorating a merchant and his wife, with a specified front dimension of the plot.

EDCS-09303042.summary: This is a simple dedication or ownership marker for Decimus Sintacius Temporis.

EDCS-09500170.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augg(ustus) Pius Felix Parthicus maximus Britannicus maximus tribunicia potestate XVI consul IIII pater patriae proconsul viam vetustate collabsam restituit curam agente Iulio Hono rato procuratore Augusti ex primipilo XI\n
EDCS-09500170.translation: The Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Pius Felix, conqueror of Parthia, conqueror of Britannia, with tribunician power for the 16th time, consul for the 4th time, father of the country, proconsul, restored the road which had collapsed from age, with the care of Julius Honoratus, procurator of Augustus, a former centurion of the 11th legion.
EDCS-09500170.summary: This imperial rescript records Emperor Marcus Aurelius restoring a road, with details of the procurator in charge of the project, likely from the late 2nd century AD.

EDCS-09500186.translation: Tiberius Caesar, son of the divine Augustus, Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 33rd year, rebuilt the road.
EDCS-09500186.summary: This inscription records Emperor Tiberius rebuilding a road, dating to his 33rd year of tribunician power (AD 32).

EDCS-09500187.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus Pius Felix [---]
EDCS-09500187.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Constantine, part of a larger inscription that is now incomplete.

EDCS-09500193.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 8th time, emperor for the 2nd time, consul for the 4th time. [---] 7
EDCS-09500193.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Antoninus Pius, listing his titles and powers, likely from around AD 145.

EDCS-09500194.translation: To the Restorer of the world, to the Emperor Caesar Lucius Domitius Aurelianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, conqueror of the Germans, conqueror of the Goths, conqueror of Parthia, with tribunician power for the 4th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, proconsul. 5
EDCS-09500194.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Aurelian, praising his military victories and imperial titles, likely from the late 3rd century AD.

EDCS-09500195.translation: To our lord Flavius Claudius Constantinus, father, grandfather, and ancestor among the emperors, born a Caesar, most noble, forever to benefit the world.
EDCS-09500195.summary: This inscription is a dedicatory text honoring Emperor Constantine, likely from the 4th century CE.

EDCS-09500199.translation: Emperor Caesar Augustus, Imperator for the 10th time, holding tribunician power for the 11th time.
EDCS-09500199.summary: This inscription records the titles and powers of an emperor, likely Augustus, dating to the early Roman Empire.

EDCS-09500202.translation: Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 8th time, Imperator for the 2nd time, Consul for the 4th time.
EDCS-09500202.summary: This inscription lists the titles and offices of Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely from the mid-2nd century CE.

EDCS-09500207.translation: Nero Claudius, son of the deified Claudius, grandson of Germanicus Caesar, great-grandson of Tiberius Caesar Augustus, great-great-grandson of the deified Augustus, Caesar Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 4th time, Imperator for the 4th time, Consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, restored (this).
EDCS-09500207.summary: This inscription details the full titulature of Emperor Nero, likely from the mid-1st century CE.

EDCS-09500208.translation: Nero Claudius, son of the deified Claudius, grandson of Germanicus Caesar, great-grandson of Tiberius Caesar Augustus, great-great-grandson of the deified Augustus, Caesar Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 4th time, Imperator for the 4th time, Consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, restored (this).
EDCS-09500208.summary: This inscription details the full titulature of Emperor Nero, likely from the mid-1st century CE.

EDCS-09500212.translation: Nero Claudius, son of the deified Claudius, grandson of Germanicus Caesar, great-grandson of Tiberius Caesar Augustus, great-great-grandson of the deified Augustus, Caesar Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 4th time, Imperator for the 4th time, Consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, restored (this).
EDCS-09500212.summary: This inscription details the full titulature of Emperor Nero, likely from the mid-1st century CE.

EDCS-09500214.translation: Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, son of Drusus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 3rd time, Consul for the 3rd time, Imperator for the 5th time, father of the fatherland, repaired (this).
EDCS-09500214.summary: This inscription records the titles and restoration work by Emperor Claudius, likely from the mid-1st century CE.

EDCS-09500215.translation: Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 9th time, Imperator for the 2nd time, Consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland, restored (this).
EDCS-09500215.summary: This inscription lists the titles and offices of Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely from the mid-2nd century CE.

EDCS-09500218.translation: Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the deified Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 22nd time, repaired and restored (this).
EDCS-09500218.summary: This inscription records the restoration work by Emperor Tiberius, likely from the early 1st century CE.

EDCS-09500228.translation: Father of the fatherland, Emperor Caesar Augustus, son of the divine, chief priest, consul for the 12th time, consul designate for the 13th time, imperator for the 14th time, holder of tribunician power for the 21st time, [year] XI.
EDCS-09500228.summary: This inscription is a titulature of Emperor Augustus, likely from a public monument, recording his many honors and offices.

EDCS-09500231.translation: Father of the fatherland, Emperor Caesar Augustus, son of the divine, chief priest, consul designate for the 13th time, imperator for the 14th time, [year] VII, [tribunician power] XI.
EDCS-09500231.summary: This inscription is a titulature of Emperor Augustus, likely from a public monument, recording his many honors and offices.

EDCS-09500232.translation: Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the divine Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 33rd time, restored and rebuilt.
EDCS-09500232.summary: This inscription records Tiberius Caesar Augustus restoring and rebuilding something, likely a public building, and indicates his 33rd year of tribunician power.

EDCS-09500235.translation: Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the divine Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 33rd time, restored and rebuilt.
EDCS-09500235.summary: This inscription records Tiberius Caesar Augustus restoring and rebuilding something, likely a public building, and indicates his 33rd year of tribunician power.

EDCS-09500240.translation: Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 8th time, consul for the 4th time, XXXVI.
EDCS-09500240.summary: This inscription is a titulature of Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely from a public monument, recording his honors and offices.

EDCS-09500242.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Domitius Aurelianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 6th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, restorer of the world, repaired and restored, XV.
EDCS-09500242.summary: This inscription is a titulature of Emperor Aurelian, likely from a public monument, recording his titles and the restoration of something.

EDCS-09500244.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Carus Pius Felix Invictus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 2nd time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul.
EDCS-09500244.summary: This inscription is a titulature of Emperor Carus, likely from a public monument, recording his titles and offices.

EDCS-09500246.translation: Father of the fatherland, Emperor Caesar Augustus, son of the divine, chief priest, consul for the 12th time, consul designate for the 13th time, imperator for the 14th time, holder of tribunician power for the 21st time.
EDCS-09500246.summary: This inscription is a titulature of Emperor Augustus, likely from a public monument, recording his many honors and offices.

EDCS-09500248.translation: Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, holder of tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 3rd time.
EDCS-09500248.summary: This inscription is a titulature of Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely from a public monument, recording his honors and offices.

EDCS-09500251.translation: To Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, with his seventh tribunician power, and third consulship.
EDCS-09500251.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely recording his seventh tribunician power and third consulship.

EDCS-09500252.translation: To Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus, pious, most noble Caesar, son of the divine Constantius Pius Augustus.
EDCS-09500252.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Constantine the Great, identifying him as the son of Constantius I and noting his status as Caesar.

EDCS-09500257.translation: To our lord Flavius Julius Constantius, most blessed Caesar, 12 miles.
EDCS-09500257.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Constantius I, likely indicating a distance of 12 miles, possibly related to a road or boundary.

EDCS-09500258.text_edition: \n Domini nostri Flavio Crispo patre avo maioribus Inpperatoribus sic nato nobilissimo Ceesari sic milia VIII Piissimis principibus Caio Valerio Constantio et Caio Valeriano Maximiano nobilissimis Caesaribus VIII\n
EDCS-09500258.translation: To our lords, Flavius Crispus, father, grandfather, and ancestor emperors, thus born most noble Caesar, 8 miles. To the most pious princes, Gaius Valerius Constantius and Gaius Valerianus Maximianus, most noble Caesars, 8.
EDCS-09500258.summary: This inscription honors multiple emperors, including Crispus and the Caesars Constantius II and Galerius Maximianus, indicating distances of 8 miles.

EDCS-09500264.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Flavio Valerio Constantino Pio Felici Augusto divi Constanti Augusti Pii filio\n
EDCS-09500264.translation: To Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus, pious, happy, unconquered Augustus, son of the divine Constantius Augustus Pius.
EDCS-09500264.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Constantine the Great, emphasizing his titles and parentage.

EDCS-09500271.translation: To Emperor Caesar Galerius Valerius Maximianus, pious, happy, unconquered Augustus, and to Maximinus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-09500271.summary: This inscription honors Emperors Galerius Maximianus and Maximinus, likely as co-rulers or in recognition of their imperial titles.

EDCS-09500301.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Magni Antonini Pii filius divi Severi nepos [Marcus Aurelius] [Antoninus] Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II consul II pater patriae proconsul milia passuum III\n
EDCS-09500301.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Antoninus Pius, grandson of the divine Severus, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with his second tribunician power, twice consul, father of the country, proconsul, 3 miles.
EDCS-09500301.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Marcus Aurelius (likely Commodus), detailing his numerous titles and a distance of 3 miles.

EDCS-09500302.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Caius Iulius Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus et Caius Iulius Maximus nobilissimus Caesar pontes et vias vetustate conlabsas sic restituerunt colonia Equestri milia passuum III\n
EDCS-09500302.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus and Gaius Julius Maximus, most noble Caesar, restored bridges and roads worn out by age, Colony of Equestri, 3 miles.
EDCS-09500302.summary: This inscription records the restoration of bridges and roads by Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son, Caesar Gaius Julius Maximus, in the colony of Equestri.

EDCS-09500303.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, chief priest, with his ninth tribunician power, emperor for the eleventh time, consul for the second time, designated for the third, father of the country, and to Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, with his fourth tribunician power, designated consul, and to Publius Septimius Geta, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-09500303.summary: This inscription honors Emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, detailing their extensive titles and powers.

EDCS-09500306.translation: To Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus, pious, happy, unconquered Augustus, and to Severus, most noble Caesar, 7.
EDCS-09500306.summary: This inscription honors Emperors Constantine the Great and Severus, likely noting a distance of 7 miles or a seventh designation.

EDCS-09500315.translation: To Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, son of Drusus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 3rd time, acclaimed imperator for the 3rd time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country. (Measurement) 7 [miles].
EDCS-09500315.summary: This is a milestone inscription from the reign of Claudius, indicating a distance of 7 miles, likely related to a road construction or maintenance project.

EDCS-09500318.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 4th time. (Measurement) 3 [miles].
EDCS-09500318.summary: This is a milestone inscription from the reign of Antoninus Pius, indicating a distance of 3 miles, likely related to a road construction or maintenance project.

EDCS-09500321.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 4th time. (Measurement) 14 [miles].
EDCS-09500321.summary: This is a milestone inscription from the reign of Antoninus Pius, indicating a distance of 14 miles, likely related to a road construction or maintenance project.

EDCS-09500322.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus Germanicus Sarmaticus Dacicus Maximus and Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar. (Measurement) 30 [miles].
EDCS-09500322.summary: This is a milestone inscription from the reign of Maximinus Thrax and his son Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, indicating a distance of 30 miles, likely related to a road construction or maintenance project.

EDCS-09500323.translation: To Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, son of Drusus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 3rd time, acclaimed imperator for the 3rd time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country. (Measurement) 25 [miles].
EDCS-09500323.summary: This is a milestone inscription from the reign of Claudius, indicating a distance of 25 miles, likely related to a road construction or maintenance project.

EDCS-09500325.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Domitius Aurelianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, greatest German, greatest Goth, greatest Carpus, holding tribunician power for the 5th time, acclaimed imperator for the 3rd time, consul, father of the country. (Measurement) 39 [miles].
EDCS-09500325.summary: This is a milestone inscription from the reign of Aurelian, indicating a distance of 39 miles, likely related to a road construction or maintenance project.

EDCS-09500326.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Claudius Pius Augustus Felix, chief priest, greatest Goth, holding tribunician power for the 3rd time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the country, proconsul.
EDCS-09500326.summary: This is a fragmented milestone inscription from the reign of Claudius Gothicus, likely indicating a distance and administrative titles.

EDCS-09500330.translation: To Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus Pius Felix Augustus, son of the divine Augustus Pius Constantine. [Text missing].
EDCS-09500330.summary: This is a milestone inscription from the reign of Constantine the Great, honoring him and his father, with a missing indication of distance or further dedication.

EDCS-09500331.translation: To our lords Dalmatins, most noble Caesar. [Text missing].
EDCS-09500331.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely dedicated to a Caesar, possibly Dalmatins, with missing context and date.

EDCS-09500332.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Domitius Aurelianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, greatest German, greatest Goth. [Text missing].
EDCS-09500332.summary: This is a fragmented milestone inscription from the reign of Aurelian, honoring him with several titles, with a missing indication of distance or further dedication.

EDCS-09500333.translation: Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the deified Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 33rd time, rebuilt and restored.
EDCS-09500333.summary: This is a dedication by Emperor Tiberius, likely for a public building or infrastructure, indicating his restoration work.

EDCS-09500334.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus, pious, fortunate, unconquered Augustus, grandson of the deified Constantius Augustus Pius, son of [---].
EDCS-09500334.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Constantine the Great, possibly on a monument commemorating his lineage and titles.

EDCS-09500335.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus, pious, most noble Caesar, son of the deified Constantius Pius Augustus. [---]
EDCS-09500335.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Constantine the Great as Caesar, likely from an earlier period of his rule, before he became Augustus.

EDCS-09500336.translation: Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the deified Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 33rd time, rebuilt and restored.
EDCS-09500336.summary: This is a dedication by Emperor Tiberius, likely for a public building or infrastructure, indicating his restoration work (variant of EDCS-09500333).

EDCS-09500340.translation: To the restorer and peacemaker of the world, the Emperor Caesar Lucius Domitius Aurelianus, pious, fortunate, unconquered Augustus, Germanicus maximus, Gothicus maximus, Carpicus maximus, Persicus maximus, pontifex maximus. [---]
EDCS-09500340.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Aurelian, listing his numerous honorific titles and acknowledging his military victories and role as a peacemaker.

EDCS-09500341.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Valerius Diocletianus, pious, fortunate, Augustus, 31 miles. // To the Emperor Caesar Licinius Licinius, 31 miles.
EDCS-09500341.summary: This inscription records milestones for roads dedicated to Emperors Diocletian and Licinius, indicating distances in Roman miles.

EDCS-09500343.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, holding tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 4th time, 17 miles.
EDCS-09500343.summary: This is a milestone inscription for Emperor Antoninus Pius, noting road distances and his consular and tribunician powers.

EDCS-09500344.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Pius, father of the fatherland, holding tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 4th time, 16 miles.
EDCS-09500344.summary: This is a milestone inscription for Emperor Antoninus Pius, noting road distances and his consular and tribunician powers.

EDCS-09500345.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Pius, father of the fatherland, holding tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 4th time, 14 miles.
EDCS-09500345.summary: This is a milestone inscription for Emperor Antoninus Pius, noting road distances and his consular and tribunician powers.

EDCS-09500346.translation: To our Lord Flavius Valerius Constantinus, most noble Caesar, Alba, 14 miles.
EDCS-09500346.summary: This is a milestone inscription likely dedicated to Constantine the Great, noting a distance from Alba.

EDCS-09500348.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Pius, father of the country, holding the tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 4th time; 9 miles.
EDCS-09500348.summary: This is a milestone inscription dedicated to Emperor Antoninus Pius, recording a distance of 9 miles and dating to his 7th tribunician power and 4th consulship.

EDCS-09500350.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Pius, father of the country, holding the tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 4th time; 6 miles.
EDCS-09500350.summary: This is a milestone inscription dedicated to Emperor Antoninus Pius, recording a distance of 6 miles and dating to his 7th tribunician power and 4th consulship.

EDCS-09500351.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Pius, father of the country, holding the tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 4th time; 4 miles.
EDCS-09500351.summary: This is a milestone inscription dedicated to Emperor Antoninus Pius, recording a distance of 4 miles and dating to his 7th tribunician power and 4th consulship.

EDCS-09500354.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Valerianus Pius Felix Augustus, father of the country, holding the tribunician power for the 3rd time, consul for the 3rd time; 14 miles.
EDCS-09500354.summary: This is a milestone inscription dedicated to Emperor Valerian, recording a distance of 14 miles and dating to his 3rd tribunician power and 3rd consulship.

EDCS-09500357.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, father of the country, consul, proconsul; Alba, 17 miles.
EDCS-09500357.summary: This is a milestone inscription dedicated to Emperor Maximian, recording a distance of 17 miles and mentioning the location Alba.

EDCS-09500360.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country, holding the tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 4th time; 10 miles.
EDCS-09500360.summary: This is a milestone inscription dedicated to Emperor Antoninus Pius, recording a distance of 10 miles and dating to his 7th tribunician power and 4th consulship.

EDCS-09500361.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus, noble Caesar, son of the divine Augustus Constantine, good for the state, born.
EDCS-09500361.summary: This inscription honors Constantine the Great's son, the Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus.

EDCS-09500362.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country, holding the tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 4th time; 13 miles (?).
EDCS-09500362.summary: This is a milestone inscription dedicated to Emperor Antoninus Pius, recording an uncertain distance of 13 miles and dating to his 7th tribunician power and 4th consulship.

EDCS-09500363.translation: To the Emperor Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country, holding the tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 4th time; 17 miles.
EDCS-09500363.summary: This is a milestone inscription dedicated to Emperor Antoninus Pius, recording a distance of 17 miles and dating to his 7th tribunician power and 4th consulship.

EDCS-09500364.translation: To the Emperor Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country, holding the tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 4th time; 20 miles.
EDCS-09500364.summary: This is a milestone inscription dedicated to Emperor Antoninus Pius, recording a distance of 20 miles and dating to his 7th tribunician power and 4th consulship.

EDCS-09500365.translation: To the Emperor Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, holding tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 4th, 21 miles.
EDCS-09500365.summary: This is an inscription marking a road of 21 miles dedicated to Emperor Antoninus Pius.
