EDCS-08401471.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Lucius Hilarianius Martidius to his dearest and incomparable wife, Maspetia Sabina.
EDCS-08401471.summary: This funerary epitaph honors Maspetia Sabina, erected by her husband Lucius Hilarianius Martidius.

EDCS-08401472.translation: Quintus Melius Flavus, son of Toutonis, for himself and for his wife, Accepta Servaci.
EDCS-08401472.summary: This funerary inscription records a tomb built by Quintus Melius Flavus for himself and his wife, Accepta Servaci.

EDCS-08401473.translation: Quintus Navicularius Victorinus to his most devoted wife, Valeria Severina.
EDCS-08401473.summary: This epitaph is a dedication by Quintus Navicularius Victorinus to his wife, Valeria Severina.

EDCS-08401476.translation: Oteia Pia, daughter of Caius, rests here.
EDCS-08401476.summary: This is a simple tomb inscription stating that Oteia Pia, daughter of Caius, is buried here.

EDCS-08401477.translation: To Peregrinus, freedman of Antistia Pia, the steward, Antistia Pia's freedwoman Cypare, to her most devoted companion.
EDCS-08401477.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Cypare, a freedwoman of Antistia Pia, to her companion Peregrinus, who was also a freedman of Antistia Pia.

EDCS-08401478.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Gentilius Persicus dedicated this under the axe to his incomparable freedwoman, Persicia Mansueta.
EDCS-08401478.summary: This funerary inscription, marked 'sub ascia', records a dedication by Gentilius Persicus to his freedwoman Persicia Mansueta.

EDCS-08401479.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Aurelius Philetus to his most devoted mother, Phila.
EDCS-08401479.summary: This funerary inscription records Aurelius Philetus's dedication to his mother, Phila.

EDCS-08401480.translation: To their sweetest daughter, Philocyrius and Acele, parents.
EDCS-08401480.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by parents for their daughter.

EDCS-08401482.translation: To the Divine Manes. Apelles writes these pious verses, bound by marriage. Death, enemy of man, snatched him away. Let these be remembered and recalled in the final hour of death. May his caring wife grant him sweet rest, as this small poem testifies. I, Sempronia, sweet, chaste, reverent to my husband, pious, dear, faithful, weep for you, my lost husband, as is just. I implore you, stone, rest lightly upon his bones, and do not wish to be a burden in my middle age. Sextus Pompeius Apelles to his incomparable wife.
EDCS-08401482.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for an incomparable wife, written by her husband Apelles, with verses lamenting her death and praising her virtues.

EDCS-08401483.translation: To the Divine Manes. To Gaius Valerius Priscianus and Aurelius Priscus, to their incomparable friend, who lived 27 years, 8 months, and 7 days.
EDCS-08401483.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Pompeius Maternus, set up by two friends who commemorate his life span and his character.

EDCS-08401484.translation: To the Divine Manes. To Marcus Pompeius Paratus, who died at the age of 5 years, 9 months, and 18 days. Pompeius Myrismus to his sweetest, most dutiful son.
EDCS-08401484.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young boy, Marcus Pompeius Paratus, set up by his father, Pompeius Myrismus.

EDCS-08401485.translation: To the Divine Manes. To Denius Staphylus's most pious wife, Pompeia Auges. To the Divine Manes. To Pompeia Tyche, alive, for herself and for her most dutiful husband, Marcus Petronius Victor.
EDCS-08401485.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording a double burial, with Pompeia Auges and Pompeia Tyche commemorated by Denius Staphylus and Marcus Petronius Victor respectively, with Tyche setting up the inscription for herself and her husband.

EDCS-08401486.translation: To Lucia Popillia, freedwoman of Lucius, Hilara.
EDCS-08401486.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucia Popillia Hilara, freedwoman of Lucius.

EDCS-08401487.translation: To the Divine Manes and memory. Precillia Themistiocla set this up for her patron, Precilius Perseus, who lived 20 years.
EDCS-08401487.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a freedwoman, Precillia Themistiocla, for her patron, Precilius Perseus, who died at the age of 20.

EDCS-08401488.translation: To the Divine Manes. The wife of Preclus Pompeianus, Chrysothemis.
EDCS-08401488.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Preclus Pompeianus, set up by his wife Chrysothemis.

EDCS-08401489.translation: To the Divine Manes. Expentanius Aquila to his incomparable wife, Praecilia Euthychia.
EDCS-08401489.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Praecilia Euthychia, set up by her husband Expentanius Aquila.

EDCS-08401490.translation: To the Divine Manes. To Quartia Heroidis, an excellent woman, who died at 22 years of age. Marcus Publius Posidonius, husband, and Publius Flavianus, son, and Quartia Aphrodisia, mother.
EDCS-08401490.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Quartia Heroidis, who died at 22, set up by her husband, son, and mother.

EDCS-08401491.translation: Quartio the pious rests here for his own.
EDCS-08401491.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Quartio, noting his piety towards his own people.

EDCS-08401492.translation: To the memory of Lucius Romanus Adrastus, from Pompeia Venusta, his dearest wife.
EDCS-08401492.summary: This inscription is a memorial dedicated by a wife, Pompeia Venusta, to her beloved husband, Lucius Romanus Adrastus.

EDCS-08401493.translation: To the spirits of the underworld for Salvia Spyches, from Salvia Fortunata her mother, to her most devoted daughter.
EDCS-08401493.summary: This is a funerary dedication from a mother, Salvia Fortunata, to her devoted daughter, Salvia Spyches.

EDCS-08401494.translation: To Gaius Samicius Octavius Romanio, from Octavia Severina, his dearest freedman, he died at the age of 18.
EDCS-08401494.summary: This tombstone records the death of Gaius Samicius Octavius Romanio, a freedman who died at 18, and was honored by Octavia Severina.

EDCS-08401495.translation: The most sweet Secundilla lies beneath this marker, who, snatched away from her parents, left behind grief, for she was as sweet as perfumes, desired always, a honeyed life, who lived for 3 years, 6 months, and 16 days. These aromatic [words].
EDCS-08401495.summary: This epitaph commemorates the tragically short life of Secundilla, who died at the age of 3 years, 6 months, and 16 days, leaving her parents in deep sorrow.

EDCS-08401496.translation: To the spirits of the underworld for Semno Felix, his most devoted husband, he died at the age of 28.
EDCS-08401496.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication from a spouse to Semno Felix, who died at the age of 28.

EDCS-08401497.translation: To the spirits of the underworld for Sempronia Tertulla, daughter of Quintus.
EDCS-08401497.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Sempronia Tertulla, daughter of Quintus.

EDCS-08401498.translation: To the spirits of the underworld for Valeria Marcia, his incomparable wife, from Sentrius Senissius.
EDCS-08401498.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by Sentrius Senissius to his incomparable wife, Valeria Marcia.

EDCS-08401499.translation: To the spirits of the underworld for Lucius Severius Primitivus, from Quintia Beronice, his dearest wife. Peace be with you. From Materna, his most dutiful wife, Marina.
EDCS-08401499.summary: This tombstone honors Lucius Severius Primitivus and includes messages from his wives, Quintia Beronice and Materna.

EDCS-08401500.translation: Mother Romula made this work of piety for you, her son Severus, and carefully founded your bones with her hand.
EDCS-08401500.summary: This epitaph records a mother, Romula, dedicating a tomb to her son Severus, highlighting her filial piety.

EDCS-08401501.translation: Here lies piety, uncertainty, sorrow, and shame, under the name Sofronius.
EDCS-08401501.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Sofronius, lamenting the ambiguous nature of piety, sorrow, and shame associated with his name.

EDCS-08401502.translation: To the departed spirits, Dapnus placed this for his wife Soteris, aged seventeen.
EDCS-08401502.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Soteris, who died at seventeen, erected by her husband Dapnus.

EDCS-08401503.translation: When Spendon had completed twice eight years, and was snatched by the Fates, Quintus Statius Murranus placed him in this tomb, his comrade, already twice.
EDCS-08401503.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Spendon, who died after completing sixteen years, and was buried by his comrade Quintus Statius Murranus.

EDCS-08401504.translation: To the departed spirits, Suallius Nicephor placed this for his most dutiful wife, Suallia Graeca.
EDCS-08401504.summary: This funerary epitaph was set up by Suallius Nicephor for his devout wife, Suallia Graeca.

EDCS-08401505.translation: To the departed spirits, Victoria placed this for her father Symmachus of Alexandria.
EDCS-08401505.summary: This funerary inscription honors Symmachus of Alexandria, dedicated by his daughter Victoria.

EDCS-08401506.translation: For Syria, daughter of Lucius, Lucilla, aged...
EDCS-08401506.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary inscription for Lucilla, daughter of Lucius and Syria, likely recording her age.

EDCS-08401507.translation: To the departed spirits, Tannonius and Valeriana, parents, placed this for their dearest son Tannonio, who lived 6 years, 6 months, and 6 days, most longed for at all times of his life.
EDCS-08401507.summary: This is a poignant funerary epitaph for Tannonio, who died at the age of six years, six months, and six days, erected by his grieving parents.

EDCS-08401508.translation: Tertulla, daughter of Chilo, made this while alive for herself and for her most sweet husband Marcus Vettius Marinus and for her son Gaius Vettius Marcellus.
EDCS-08401508.summary: This is a funerary monument commissioned during her lifetime by Tertulla for herself, her husband Marcus Vettius Marinus, and her son Gaius Vettius Marcellus.

EDCS-08401509.translation: To the departed spirits, for Gaius Tettius, son of Gaius, Julius Finanus, aged twenty-three.
EDCS-08401509.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Gaius Tettius Iulius Finanus at the age of twenty-three.

EDCS-08401510.translation: To the departed spirits, Marcus Titius Marcellinus and Titia Marciana placed this for their most dutiful father, Marcus Titus Hermetis.
EDCS-08401510.summary: This funerary epitaph was erected by Marcus Titius Marcellinus and Titia Marciana for their pious father, Marcus Titius Hermetis.

EDCS-08401511.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Caius Cosius Trophimas to his most dutiful mother Titia Augeni.
EDCS-08401511.summary: This is a funerary dedication for a mother, made by her son.

EDCS-08401512.translation: Nicia, freedwoman of [---], made this while alive for herself and her patroness Turrania, freedwoman of Sextus.
EDCS-08401512.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by a freedwoman for herself and her patroness.

EDCS-08401513.translation: To Pax Turrania Pithanes, to her most sweet husband.
EDCS-08401513.summary: This is a funerary dedication for a husband, made by his wife.

EDCS-08401514.translation: To Lucius Valerius Asticus, from his most dutiful wife Aemilia Chrysopolis, and to his most dutiful uncle, Marcus Iulius Quintilianus.
EDCS-08401514.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Lucius Valerius Asticus, made by his wife and uncle.

EDCS-08401515.translation: The mother who bore Caius Valerius Didymionis, born on the same day, who shared the same hour of birth, was taken by the gods according to fate and her eternal husband.
EDCS-08401515.summary: This inscription laments the death of a mother who died around the time of her children's birth.

EDCS-08401516.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Gaius Valerius Didymus set this up for himself while alive.
EDCS-08401516.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by a man for himself.

EDCS-08401517.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Valerius Didymus the younger, who lived 18 years, 9 months, and 7 days. Valerius Didymus, his most unhappy father, set this up for his son.
EDCS-08401517.summary: This is a funerary dedication for a young man, made by his grieving father.

EDCS-08401518.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Titus Valerius Dionysus, from Valeria Charis his wife, and Valerius Marcellus and Felicio, to their most dutiful father.
EDCS-08401518.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Titus Valerius Dionysus, made by his wife and sons.

EDCS-08401519.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Gaius Valerius Philocrates set this up for himself while alive.
EDCS-08401519.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by a man for himself.

EDCS-08401521.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Lucius Valerius Servandus, son of Lucius, to his most dutiful husband.
EDCS-08401521.summary: This is a funerary dedication for a husband, made by his wife.

EDCS-08401522.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Valeria Lucilla, daughter of Lucius, and for Protoctetus, her most devoted comrade, and for Eutychia.
EDCS-08401522.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Valeria Lucilla, her comrade Protoctetus, and Eutychia.

EDCS-08401523.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Valeria Saturnina, daughter of Lucius, who died at 21 years of age; her unhappy mother.
EDCS-08401523.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Valeria Saturnina at the age of 21, set up by her mother.

EDCS-08401524.translation: For Valeria Secundio, Lucius Popilius Clemens to his devoted wife.
EDCS-08401524.summary: Lucius Popilius Clemens dedicated this monument to his devoted wife, Valeria Secundio.

EDCS-08401525.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Valeria Thallusa, Valeria Eutyches to her most devoted mother and Manlius Hermes to his incomparable mother-in-law.
EDCS-08401525.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by Valeria Eutyches to her mother Valeria Thallusa and by Manlius Hermes to his mother-in-law.

EDCS-08401526.summary: This inscription is a dedication to an unnamed 'Valeria', identified as a most devoted mother.

EDCS-08401528.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Verconnia Glyce, his most devoted wife, who died; Ephebus and his family.
EDCS-08401528.summary: Ephebus and his family dedicated this funerary inscription to his devoted wife, Verconnia Glyce.

EDCS-08401529.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Verria Pareta, who died at 13 years, 2 months, and 18 days; for her most devoted daughter, Verria Chrysophania.
EDCS-08401529.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Verria Pareta at the age of 13 years, 2 months, and 18 days, and is dedicated by her daughter, Verria Chrysophania.

EDCS-08401530.summary: This is a dedication to Marcus Vibius Agathemerus.

EDCS-08401531.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For the most devoted Victorina. Crassius Euplastus to his dearest wife and to his son Crassius Euhodian, who...
EDCS-08401531.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by Crassius Euplastus to his wife Victorina and son Crassius Euhodian.

EDCS-08401532.translation: Lucius Virius Fortunatus and Thais made (this) for their children while alive.
EDCS-08401532.summary: Lucius Virius Fortunatus and Thais made this monument for their children during their lifetime.

EDCS-08401533.translation: Vitalis, son of Marci Verati Clari, lived 5 years. He is laid here. I inspected the light, which was suddenly snatched from me. Thus, I was not allowed to take joy from my master, nor to know what I was born for.
EDCS-08401533.summary: This epitaph laments the premature death of a young boy named Vitalis, who died at the age of 5.

EDCS-08401534.translation: To Voturia Elpis, who lived 40 years, 3 months, and 7 days. For 4 days, she was between two wives and a husband. Lucceius Eutices set this up for his most deserving patroness.
EDCS-08401534.summary: This epitaph records the death of Voturia Elpis, noting her age and the period she was between two wives and a husband, set up by Lucceius Eutices.

EDCS-08401535.translation: To Zosimus, for his most dutiful mother.
EDCS-08401535.summary: This is a simple funerary dedication from Zosimus to his devoted mother.

EDCS-08401537.translation: To Arba Suru, a freedwoman. Her bones are in this tomb; her ashes are elsewhere. I built this.
EDCS-08401537.summary: This epitaph indicates that the remains of Arba Suru, a freedwoman, are interred here, though her ashes are elsewhere, with the monument constructed by an unnamed individual.

EDCS-08401549.translation: Son of Titus, heirs. From the will.
EDCS-08401549.summary: This inscription indicates that the property or estate belongs to the heirs of Titus, as stipulated in his will.

EDCS-08401560.summary: This very brief inscription records an action by Aphrodisius, who redeemed 'kings', the context of which is unclear.

EDCS-08401561.translation: Here rests in peace Eustasia of good memory, who lived 36 years, died on the 4th day before the Nones of September, in the consulship of Avienus.
EDCS-08401561.summary: This epitaph records the death of Eustasia, noting her age, date of death, and the consul Avienus, placing it in his consular year.

EDCS-08401562.translation: Here rests in peace Sofronius of good memory, who lived 54 years, died on the 12th day before the Kalends of November, in the consulship of Viator, a most distinguished man.
EDCS-08401562.summary: This epitaph records the death of Sofronius, noting his age, date of death, and the consul Viator, placing it in his consular year.

EDCS-08401564.translation: Here rests in peace Eulogios of good memory, who lived more or less 50 years, and died on the 8th day before the Kalends of August, in the 7th indiction, again after the consulship of Symmachus, a most distinguished man.
EDCS-08401564.summary: This epitaph records the death of Eulogios, noting his approximate age, date of death, the 7th indiction, and referencing a second consulship of Symmachus.

EDCS-08401565.translation: Here rests in peace Paulus of good memory, who lived more or less 42 years, and died on the day before the Nones of January, in the seventh indiction, again after the consulship of Mavortius.
EDCS-08401565.summary: This epitaph records the death of Paulus, noting his approximate age, date of death, the seventh indiction, and referencing a second consulship of Mavortius.

EDCS-08401566.translation: Apriles of good memory rests here in peace, who lived more or less 40 years and died on the tenth day before the Kalends of November, in the ninth indiction, when Lampadius and Orestes, most distinguished men, were consuls.
EDCS-08401566.summary: This epitaph records the death of Apriles, who lived approximately 40 years, with the date and consuls provided.

EDCS-08401567.translation: Petrus, son of the late Asclepius, of good memory rests here in peace. He founded this basilica of Saints Peter and Paul, lived more or less 43 years, and died on the 14th day before the Kalends of February, in the eighth indiction, after the consulship of Decitius Iunior, a most distinguished man.
EDCS-08401567.summary: This epitaph records the death of Petrus, son of Asclepius, who founded a basilica and lived about 43 years, providing the date and consul.

EDCS-08401568.translation: Secolasia of good memory rests here in peace, who lived 46 years and ten days; she died on the sixth day before the Kalends of March, after the consulship of Lampadius and Orestes, consular men.
EDCS-08401568.summary: This epitaph records the death of Secolasia, who lived 46 years and 10 days, with the date and consuls specified.

EDCS-08401569.translation: Thaumasta of good memory rests here in peace, who lived 60 years and died on the day before the Kalends of December, in the eleventh indiction, again after the consulship of Lampadius and Orestes, most distinguished men.
EDCS-08401569.summary: This epitaph records the death of Thaumasta, who lived 60 years, with the date and consuls provided.

EDCS-08401570.translation: He lived 36 years and died on the day before the Nones of September, in the fifth indiction, under Basilius, a most distinguished man.
EDCS-08401570.summary: This epitaph fragment records the death of an individual who lived 36 years, with the date and a consul mentioned.

EDCS-08401571.translation: Antoninus of good memory rests here in peace, who lived more or less 45 years and died on the second day of [---].
EDCS-08401571.summary: This epitaph records the death of Antoninus, who lived about 45 years, with the date partially preserved.

EDCS-08401572.translation: Benenata of good memory rests here in peace, who lived 2 years and 38 days; she died on the day of the Kalends of August, the festival of the saints.
EDCS-08401572.summary: This epitaph records the death of Benenata, who lived 2 years and 38 days, with the date specified.

EDCS-08401573.translation: Concordius, whose life was whole and body pure, lives eternally here, having been placed in the everlasting age. He shone first as a minister in his tender years, then was chosen by heavenly law as a priest; he returned the thirty-second year of his life to God. This omnipotent one, snatched away to the starry court, is sought by his mother Blanda and his brother, though without funeral rites.
EDCS-08401573.summary: This epitaph praises the deceased Concordius, a priest and minister who died at 32, and mentions his grieving mother and brother.

EDCS-08401575.translation: Seeking the bright kingdoms, called by a heavenly fate, joyfully entering the heights of light and the ether, the excellent and pious Florentinus now shines in this tomb, most holy abbot through his merits. He once trod down the dangers of speech, and neither sought to wish evil nor to harm anyone; yet he nevertheless struck fear with his easy public words, always observing the path of justice with paternal peace, revealing straightforward conversations to all, and from his whole heart he promptly uttered words in which the Lord's proclamations always resound, and with incessant hymns he applauds the heavens. He waged war against the pestilential vices of the flesh and openly fought most savage battles, and conquered. Henceforth, happy, he takes the high rewards of heaven, and enjoys the crown with praise as a companion of the saints. For the body of this man, consecrated throughout, was brought long ago with great praise to the Lord's presence. The abbot Constantinus joyfully and auspiciously placed it within the sacred heights of the blessed cross, and also strengthening the precious coverings of the tomb, he constructed a support from solid, crust-like marble. However, after seven decades had been completed, the remains which had been interred in the aforementioned tomb finally emerged from the blessed seat of Peter, which now display signs of salvation here through their own merits, and simultaneously offer the benefits of virtues and large comforts, giving robust vigor to the infirm at all times. Therefore, mighty shepherd, compensate his vows with rewards. Often commend the poet to Christ, whose small poems promised you praise – Ianuarius, whom you nurtured with a pure mind, and also the sculptor of this marble. With your accustomed manner, through constant prayers to the Lord for all ages, commend him gently and perpetually. And now you know him to be a monk, whom you, holy and kind, now and always adorn as Hilarianus in eternity. Thus, Saint Florentinus was the first abbot of our monastery for 5 years and 6 months; he lived more or less 70 years, and died the day before the Ides of April, twelve years after the consulship of Basilius Iunior, a most distinguished man, in the first indiction. After him, the second was the lord abbot Redemptus.
EDCS-08401575.summary: This lengthy inscription commemorates Abbot Florentinus, highlighting his virtues, piety, and battles against sin, and details the rebuilding and transference of his tomb by Abbot Constantinus, mentioning Ianuarius as the poet and sculptor and Hilarianus as another monk, finally noting Florentinus as the first abbot and Redemptus as his successor.

EDCS-08401576.translation: Fortunatus of good memory rests here in peace, who lived more or less 60 years and died on the 5th day before the Ides of January, in the twelfth indiction.
EDCS-08401576.summary: This epitaph records the death of Fortunatus, who lived approximately 60 years, with the date and indiction specified.

EDCS-08401577.translation: Sanctus Heros, the highest priest, died.
EDCS-08401577.summary: This inscription records the death of Sanctus Heros, identified as a high priest.

EDCS-08401579.translation: Here rests in peace Iohannula of good memory, who lived approximately 20 years and died on the 8th day before the Ides of November, in the 15th indiction.
EDCS-08401579.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Iohannula, providing her approximate age and date of death.

EDCS-08401581.translation: Hilarius, priest of the most holy law, rests here, a priest of the Lord who, preferring the love of poverty to gold, seized the heavenly kingdom. Hilarius, for whom Christ was life, disdained earthly things, leaving behind the spoil of his flesh, flying to the stars. He scorned riches while seeking riches, changing mortal things, and bought heaven with eternal gifts. He was a jewel of priests, a master of the people and the world, also taking on humble duties for Christ, not disdaining to perform acts of service as a servant. He lived as the least and was the highest in rank. Nor is it surprising that after these deeds he deserved your thresholds, Christ, and entered the angelic homes and golden kingdoms, seeing your abundant paradisiacal riches, your ever-burning meadows, and gardens fragrant with divine flowers, the clouds below, and the stars of heaven.
EDCS-08401581.summary: This is a lengthy epitaph for Hilarius, a priest who is praised for his piety, poverty, and devotion to Christ, describing his virtuous life and heavenly reward.

EDCS-08401582.translation: Here rests in peace Marsiola of good memory, who lived 50 years and died on the day before the Ides of April.
EDCS-08401582.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Marsiola, providing her age and date of death.

EDCS-08401583.translation: Here rests in peace Marta of good memory, who lived approximately 35 years and died on the 7th day before the Kalends of October, in the 6th indiction.
EDCS-08401583.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Marta, providing her approximate age and date of death.

EDCS-08401584.translation: Here rests in peace Martha of good memory, who lived approximately 6 years and died on the 5th day before the Kalends of December.
EDCS-08401584.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Martha, providing her approximate age and date of death.

EDCS-08401585.translation: Here rests in peace, in peace, Mansuetus of good memory with his daughter Blandola, who lived approximately 45 years and died on the 3rd day before the Nones of September, in the fifth indiction.
EDCS-08401585.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Mansuetus and his daughter Blandola, providing their approximate ages and date of death.

EDCS-08401586.translation: Here rests in peace Melania of good memory, who lived approximately 14 years and died on the 13th day before the Kalends of December, in the 6th indiction.
EDCS-08401586.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Melania, providing her approximate age and date of death.

EDCS-08401587.translation: Here rests in peace Neclicta of good memory, who lived approximately 27 years and died on the tenth day before the Kalends of September.
EDCS-08401587.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Neclicta, providing her approximate age and date of death.

EDCS-08401588.translation: Ennius Filterius, or Pompeius, to his most loving wife Optatina Reticia, also called Pascasia, erected this tomb; he lived with her for eight years, nine months, and two days.
EDCS-08401588.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Ennius Filterius (or Pompeius) for his wife Optatina Reticia (or Pascasia), indicating their lifespan together.

EDCS-08401589.translation: Here rests in peace Procula of good memory, who lived more than 55 years and died on the Nones of October.
EDCS-08401589.summary: This is a tombstone epitaph recording the death and age of Procula, dating from antiquity.

EDCS-08401590.translation: May peace be with you, Proiecta, Lampadia, and Renatus.
EDCS-08401590.summary: This is a short funerary inscription invoking peace for Proiecta, Lampadia, and Renatus.

EDCS-08401592.translation: Here sleeps in peace Secunda, who lived 13 years, 8 months, and 22 days, laid to rest on the 6th day before the Ides of March, [by her] parents, for their dearest daughter.
EDCS-08401592.summary: This is a tombstone epitaph for Secunda, detailing her age at death and burial date, placed by her parents.

EDCS-08401593.translation: Here lies Silvina, innocent, approved by her husband, who lived to be fifteen years and the ninth day of March, and was placed in this tomb. Her father, looking back on her life and merit, [placed] this vessel containing her body near a holy martyr.
EDCS-08401593.summary: This is a tombstone epitaph for Silvina, recording her life, death date, and her father's devotion.

EDCS-08401594.translation: Here rests in peace Sybilla of good memory, who lived and died.
EDCS-08401594.summary: This is a tombstone epitaph for Sybilla, noting her rest in peace and her life and death.

EDCS-08401597.translation: In blessed memory of devoted services, to her well-deserving husband Victorinus, who lived 60 years, Proiecta his wife placed [this]. Resting in peace on the 4th day before the Nones of September, with whom she lived 4 years and 3 months.
EDCS-08401597.summary: This is a tombstone epitaph placed by Proiecta for her husband Victorinus, detailing his age and their marital duration.

EDCS-08401598.translation: Here rests in peace Benenorius Vitalis, who lived 32 years.
EDCS-08401598.summary: This is a tombstone epitaph for Benenorius Vitalis, stating his name, resting place, and age at death.

EDCS-08401599.translation: Here rests in peace Ursula of good memory, who lived more or less 30 years and died on the 5th day before the Kalends of July in the fifth indiction.
EDCS-08401599.summary: This is a tombstone epitaph for Ursula, recording her age at death, date of death, and the indiction period.

EDCS-08401601.translation: Here rests in peace the divine grandson of Saint Caesarius, the bishop, who lived more or less.
EDCS-08401601.summary: This is a tombstone epitaph for the grandson of Saint Caesarius, a bishop, noting his lineage and life.

EDCS-08500450.summary: This is a very short inscription possibly related to a burial or a place struck by lightning.

EDCS-08500452.translation: Son of Somnius, son of Ugenon, son of Iones.
EDCS-08500452.summary: This inscription is a brief, possibly funerary, epitaph indicating the patronymic lineage of an individual.

EDCS-08500459.translation: Patedna, together with his three family members.
EDCS-08500459.summary: This inscription likely records a dedication or funerary marker for Patedna and three other individuals, possibly family members.

EDCS-08500655.translation: Lucius Cellius Stefanus placed this for his most devoted mother, Cellia Athenaïs.
EDCS-08500655.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Lucius Cellius Stefanus for his mother, Cellia Athenaïs.

EDCS-08500656.summary: This appears to be a funerary inscription, though incomplete, indicating that Caldus, son of someone, set it up.

EDCS-08500657.translation: Erimada, daughter of Tleisa, daughter of Votonna, and daughter of Montanus, devoutly placed this.
EDCS-08500657.summary: This inscription is a dedication, possibly funerary, by Erimada, who identifies herself through her paternal lineage and also names Montanus.

EDCS-08500658.summary: This is a simple inscription, likely a name on a tomb or monument, identifying Marcus Licinius Aereptus.

EDCS-08500659.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Iulia Nice placed this for her husband, Marcus Frontonius Euporus, a distinguished member of the Augustales, a maritime shipowner from Arelate, curator of the same guild, and patron of the guild of boatmen of the Druentius and the guild of leatherworkers of Ernaginum, as her dearest husband.
EDCS-08500659.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Frontonius Euporus, a prominent guild leader and shipowner from Arelate, dedicated by his wife Iulia Nice.

EDCS-08500660.translation: Marcus Severius Viator, son of Marcus, of the Fabian tribe, a flamen of Rome and the Augusti, a duovir, and pontifex of the colony of Reii Apollinaris, made this for himself and for Kareia Patercla, his excellent wife.
EDCS-08500660.summary: This is a funerary or commemorative inscription commissioned by Marcus Severius Viator, an important local official, for himself and his wife.

EDCS-08500661.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Varius Festus dedicated this for his most sweet wife, Valeria Pia, under the axe.
EDCS-08500661.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by Varius Festus to his wife, Valeria Pia, with the dedication 'under the axe' signifying a burial.

EDCS-08500662.translation: For Publius Caecilius, son of Publius, of the Pupinian tribe, from Baeterrae in the province of Narbonensis, a soldier of the First Minervia and the Sixth Victrix legions.
EDCS-08500662.summary: This inscription records a dedication or funerary marker for Publius Caecilius, a soldier serving in two Roman legions.

EDCS-08500663.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Marcus Cornelius Symbiotes to his friend Lucius Aemilius Tryphon.
EDCS-08500663.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication by Marcus Cornelius Symbiotes to his deceased friend Lucius Aemilius Tryphon.

EDCS-08500664.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Quintus Caprius Hermes.
EDCS-08500664.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication to Quintus Caprius Hermes.

EDCS-08500665.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Gaius Secundius Iulianus to his excellent wife Catullia Quinta.
EDCS-08500665.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication by Gaius Secundius Iulianus to his wife Catullia Quinta.

EDCS-08500666.translation: Titus Cornelius Planta, by his will, ordered this to be made for himself and his son Lucius Cornelius Laetus.
EDCS-08500666.summary: This inscription records a posthumous commission by Titus Cornelius Planta for a monument to be erected for himself and his son Lucius Cornelius Laetus.

EDCS-08500667.translation: To the spirits of the departed: the heirs by will erected this for Maecia Vetuscula, daughter of Lucius, in accordance with the will.
EDCS-08500667.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication by the heirs of Maecia Vetuscula, daughter of Lucius, to her spirits.

EDCS-08500670.translation: To Good Fortune: Carsia Udia, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-08500670.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Carsia Udia to the goddess Bona Fortuna.

EDCS-08500671.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the Protector: Decimus A. Attianus.
EDCS-08500671.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Decimus A. Attianus to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Conservator.

EDCS-08500672.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the Protector: Sextus Julius Luto dedicated this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-08500672.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Sextus Julius Luto to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Conservator.

EDCS-08500674.translation: To Luna the Light-Bringer: Attia Anatolia.
EDCS-08500674.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Attia Anatolia to the goddess Luna Lucifera.

EDCS-08500675.translation: Sextus Valerius Samicus, son of Lutevius, paid his vow to Minerva willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-08500675.summary: This inscription records Sextus Valerius Samicus fulfilling a vow to Minerva.

EDCS-08500677.translation: Aulus A. A. gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus.
EDCS-08500677.summary: An altar dedicated to Silvanus by Aulus A. A.

EDCS-08500678.summary: A dedication to the god Silvanus by Helvius.

EDCS-08500679.translation: Lucius Vibius Montanus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow for his freedman Amerimnus.
EDCS-08500679.summary: A votive inscription by Lucius Vibius Montanus for his freedman Amerimnus.

EDCS-08500680.translation: Decimus A. Eudaemon to Sol Invictus.
EDCS-08500680.summary: A dedication to the unconquered sun god Sol Invictus by Decimus A. Eudaemon.

EDCS-08500682.translation: To the Spirits of the departed and to eternal memory, Aebutia Eutychia for her patron, most devoted towards her, Agathon, formerly an Augustalis corporatus of the colony of Iulia Paterna Arelas, twice curator of the same guild, also an Augustalis of the colony of Iulia Aptae, a bargeman of the Arar, curator of the common fund of the Glanici, who lived 70 years.
EDCS-08500682.summary: A tombstone for Agathon, a prominent local official and businessman, set up by his devoted patron Aebutia Eutychia.

EDCS-08500683.translation: To the Spirits of the departed, Acilia Charile for her well-deserving fellow freedman Acilius Elpidephorus.
EDCS-08500683.summary: A tombstone inscription for Acilius Elpidephorus, dedicated by his fellow freedwoman Acilia Charile.

EDCS-08500684.translation: Maxsumus Matislius for himself and his family, 10 feet wide.
EDCS-08500684.summary: A funerary marker indicating the dimensions of a plot for Maxsumus Matislius and his family.

EDCS-08500685.translation: To the memory of Aurelius Gratinus, who lived 60 years, 11 months, and [X] days, to his most devoted [son/relative?] Aur. N. TAI.
EDCS-08500685.summary: A memorial inscription for Aurelius Gratinus, with the dedication likely by a relative.

EDCS-08500687.translation: To the Spirits of the departed, Quartina, daughter of Deccia Cratilla, and Caius Primus for their most beloved wife.
EDCS-08500687.summary: A tombstone inscription for a wife, dedicated by her daughter and husband.

EDCS-08500688.translation: To the Spirits of the departed, Maticia for her most devoted husband, Quintus Frontonius Ianuarius Nason.
EDCS-08500688.summary: A tombstone inscription for Quintus Frontonius Ianuarius Nason, dedicated by his devoted wife Maticia.

EDCS-08500689.translation: Sextus Lucius Marcus, sons of Julius, to their parents.
EDCS-08500689.summary: This funerary inscription records a dedication made by Sextus Lucius Marcus to their parents.

EDCS-08500690.translation: Gaius Licinius Nothus, freedman of Gaius, for himself and for Licinia Auga, a fellow freedwoman.
EDCS-08500690.summary: This funerary inscription records a dedication made by Gaius Licinius Nothus for himself and his fellow freedwoman Licinia Auga.

EDCS-08500691.translation: To the Spirits of the departed, Marcia Lamyra set this up for her most beloved husband, Lucius Nasonius Primitivus.
EDCS-08500691.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication of a monument by Marcia Lamyra to her husband, Lucius Nasonius Primitivus.

EDCS-08500692.translation: To the Spirits of the departed, for Pompeia Nympha, their most dutiful mother.
EDCS-08500692.summary: This funerary inscription records a dedication to Pompeia Nympha, identified as a most dutiful mother.

EDCS-08500693.translation: [...]atio Solimutus, to his son, and Gaius Julius.
EDCS-08500693.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records a dedication or memorial for Solimutus' son and Gaius Julius.

EDCS-08500694.translation: Marcus to his son MAXI HILAR.
EDCS-08500694.summary: This funerary inscription records a dedication by Marcus to his son, MAXI HILAR.

EDCS-08500695.translation: Lucius Valerius to his wife Quartae.
EDCS-08500695.summary: This funerary inscription records a dedication by Lucius Valerius to his wife Quartae.

EDCS-08500696.translation: Valeria, freedwoman of Lucius, to her son Quintus Veratius.
EDCS-08500696.summary: This funerary inscription records a dedication by Valeria, a freedwoman of Lucius, to her son Quintus Veratius.

EDCS-08500697.translation: To the Spirits of the departed, Titus Vibius Valerius to his most dutiful father, Titus Vibius Secundinus.
EDCS-08500697.summary: This funerary inscription records a dedication by Titus Vibius Valerius to his father, Titus Vibius Secundinus.

EDCS-08500701.summary: This is a simple epitaph stating that Stefanus rests in this tomb.

EDCS-08500702.translation: Sextus Veratius Pothus, freedman of Prisca, gladly and deservedly paid his vow to Copia.
EDCS-08500702.summary: This is a dedication by a freedman to Copia, likely a goddess, expressing gratitude for a fulfilled vow.

EDCS-08500704.translation: The city dwellers and household servants of Attalus and Firmus gladly and deservedly paid their vow to Silvanus.
EDCS-08500704.summary: This is a dedication to Silvanus by a group of people, possibly colleagues or household members, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-08500707.translation: Titus Carisius, son of Titus, the praetor, gives this to the Volcae.
EDCS-08500707.summary: This inscription records a gift from Titus Carisius, identified as a praetor, to the Volcae, likely a local tribe.

EDCS-08500708.translation: To Gaius Otacilius Oppianus, son of Gaius, of the Voltinia tribe, duovir.
EDCS-08500708.summary: This is an inscription honoring Gaius Otacilius Oppianus, identified by his tribe and a magistracy, likely an honorary dedication.

EDCS-08500709.summary: This is a fragmentary dedication, possibly to a deity or notable individual, with the name Ateuloibitos and filiation.

EDCS-08500713.translation: Atia, daughter of Sextus, of the Voltinia tribe, wife of Aulus.
EDCS-08500713.summary: This inscription records the names and affiliations of Atia, likely indicating her family and marital status.

EDCS-08500714.summary: This is a simple inscription recording the name and filiation of Lucius Cornelius.

EDCS-08500715.translation: To the departed spirits, Ianuarius Primitivus has given this memorial to his wife Cupitia Florentina, a pious and chaste wife, such as poverty could provide.
EDCS-08500715.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by a husband for his deceased wife, lamenting his limited means.

EDCS-08500716.translation: To the departed spirits, Gaius Iulius Alexander [set this up] for his dearest wife Egnatia Materna.
EDCS-08500716.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by a husband for his deceased wife, expressing affection.

EDCS-08500717.translation: Vales Gabinius, greetings. Valerius Gabinius is buried here.
EDCS-08500717.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Valerius Gabinius, written in a mix of Latin and Greek.

EDCS-08500718.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Sextus Julius Cornelianus, son of Sextus, of the Voltinian tribe, aged 22. Quintus Cornelius Eutyches and Cornelia Apolauste [set this up] for their best friend and parents for their most devoted son.
EDCS-08500718.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by parents for their deceased son, Sextus Julius Cornelianus, who died at the age of 22.

EDCS-08500719.translation: Homullus, [his] son, [for] Licinia, daughter of Marcus.
EDCS-08500719.summary: This is a funerary epitaph identifying Homullus as the son of Licinia, daughter of Marcus.

EDCS-08500720.translation: Publius Maximius Victor [set this up] for Maximilla, daughter of Statutus.
EDCS-08500720.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Publius Maximius Victor for Maximilla, daughter of Statutus.

EDCS-08500721.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Titus Navi, a freedman.

EDCS-08500722.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Caius Valerius Inachus set this up during his lifetime for his most precious wife, Pompeia Pia, and for himself.
EDCS-08500722.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Caius Valerius Inachus for his wife Pompeia Pia and for himself.

EDCS-08500727.translation: Enclosing the limits of life with months and twins, she lives forever, destined never to die throughout eternity. Thus resolved she asked, obeying the laws of the priesthood and the conjugal name. She died, good Casaria, in the middle of the night, as the day of the Lord was dawning, on the 8th day before the Ides of December, in the 46th year, after the consulship of the most illustrious consul Basilius Iunior, in the 12th year of the reign of King Childebert, in the fifth indiction.
EDCS-08500727.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Casaria, recording her death on December 5th in the 46th year of her life, within the 12th regnal year of King Childebert and the fifth indiction.

EDCS-08500730.translation: To Jupiter. Marcus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-08500730.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter by Marcus.

EDCS-08500731.translation: In accordance with the vow, Pompeius Bithus.
EDCS-08500731.summary: This is a votive inscription stating that Pompeius Bithus fulfilled a vow.

EDCS-08500732.translation: To Lucius Julius Burus, son of Decimus, of the Voltinian tribe, a camp prefect, and a comrade.
EDCS-08500732.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Julius Burus, son of Decimus, who held the office of a prefect.

EDCS-08500733.translation: Aulus Pompeius Fronto, son of Sextus, again; Sextus Lucceius Aelianus, son of Aulus; Decimus Julius Labeo, son of Decimus; Caius Areius Fronto, son of Cai. The four magistrates arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-08500733.summary: This inscription records that four magistrates, Aulus Pompeius Fronto, Sextus Lucceius Aelianus, Decimus Julius Labeo, and Caius Areius Fronto, commissioned a work.

EDCS-08500734.translation: Aulus Vercius Lausus, one of the six Augustales priests, made this for himself and his family, for his patron Aulus Vercius Iucundus.
EDCS-08500734.summary: This is a dedication made by Aulus Vercius Lausus for his patron, likely an honorific monument or a funerary one.

EDCS-08500735.summary: This is a very short funerary inscription, likely a tombstone, indicating parentage.

EDCS-08500736.translation: Marcus Maccius Festus and Sextus Maccius Severus, for Sextus Cornelius Vernas.
EDCS-08500736.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or a memorial possibly by Marcus Maccius Festus and Sextus Maccius Severus for Sextus Cornelius Vernas.

EDCS-08500737.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a name on a tombstone or monument.

EDCS-08500739.translation: Hilarus rests here, who died on the eleventh day before the Kalends of May.
EDCS-08500739.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the name and date of death of Hilarus.

EDCS-08500741.translation: Albiorica, willingly and deservedly, paid [her] vow.
EDCS-08500741.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Albiorica, indicating a vow fulfilled.

EDCS-08500742.translation: Gaius L. Calvo, for Bergonia, willingly and deservedly, paid [his] vow.
EDCS-08500742.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Bergonia by Gaius L. Calvo, indicating a vow fulfilled.

EDCS-08500743.translation: Aulus Cominius Successus, for Dexsiva, willingly and deservedly, paid [his] vow.
EDCS-08500743.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Dexsiva by Aulus Cominius Successus, indicating a vow fulfilled.

EDCS-08500744.translation: Quartus gave an axe as a gift to Mars; Quartus gave an axe as a gift. For Dexsiva, Quartus willingly and deservedly paid [his] vow.
EDCS-08500744.summary: This inscription records a vow paid by Quartus to Mars and Dexsiva, including the dedication of an axe.

EDCS-08500745.translation: Caius Helvius Primus, for Dexiva and the people of Caudium, willingly and deservedly, paid [his] vow, [dedicating] seats.
EDCS-08500745.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated by Caius Helvius Primus to Dexiva and the Caudenses, likely for a public benefit such as seating.

EDCS-08500746.translation: Quintus Cornelius Smertullus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to his brother Placidus.
EDCS-08500746.summary: This is a votive inscription by Quintus Cornelius Smertullus to his brother Placidus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500747.translation: Marcus Erucius Natalis gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter the Preserver of all things.
EDCS-08500747.summary: This is a votive inscription by Marcus Erucius Natalis to Jupiter Conservator, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500750.translation: Caius Iulius V Avenniensis gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-08500750.summary: This is a votive inscription by Caius Iulius V Avenniensis to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500751.translation: Caius Attius Sequens to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-08500751.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Caius Attius Sequens, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500753.translation: Sextus Solimarius Frontinus to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-08500753.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Sextus Solimarius Frontinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500754.translation: Lucius Vinicius Verinus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-08500754.summary: This is a votive inscription by Lucius Vinicius Verinus to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500755.translation: Quintus Iulius M gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest, the Preserver.
EDCS-08500755.summary: This is a votive inscription by Quintus Iulius M to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Conservator, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500756.translation: Marcus V S gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Father Liber.
EDCS-08500756.summary: This is a votive inscription by Marcus V S to Liber Pater, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500757.translation: Marcus Taminius Mansuetus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Mars.
EDCS-08500757.summary: This is a votive inscription by Marcus Taminius Mansuetus to Mars, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500758.translation: Reppavi, son of Vectirix, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Mars.
EDCS-08500758.summary: This is a votive inscription by Reppavi to Mars, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500759.translation: Marcus Attius Karus [dedicated this] to the Mothers for his brother.
EDCS-08500759.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Marcus Attius Karus to the Matres (Mothers) for his brother.

EDCS-08500762.translation: Lucius Coelius Surillio, a slave, willingly paid his vow to Mercury.
EDCS-08500762.summary: This inscription records a vow paid by the slave Lucius Coelius Surillio to the god Mercury.

EDCS-08500763.translation: Lucius Iuventius Ingenuus, Donnius Saturninus, Sextus Iulius Maximus, Aulus Iulius Pergamus, Marcus Pompeius Primitivos, Valerius Maternus, and Rimmius Augurinus willingly paid their vow to Mercury, the god of the Vogienses and of the merchants of the centesima and rotari taxes.
EDCS-08500763.summary: This inscription records a vow paid by a group of men, including tax collectors, to Mercury, patron god of the Vogienses tribe and of specific taxes.

EDCS-08500764.translation: Secundina, daughter of Primulus, willingly and deservedly paid her vow to Mercury.
EDCS-08500764.summary: This inscription records a vow paid by Secundina, daughter of Primulus, to the god Mercury.

EDCS-08500765.translation: Fronto Kapinius willingly and deservedly paid his vow to Mercury Veator.
EDCS-08500765.summary: This inscription records a vow paid by Fronto Kapinius to Mercury Veator, an epithet of Mercury associated with travel or speed.

EDCS-08500766.translation: Mullius and his associates willingly and deservedly paid their vow in the hunt.
EDCS-08500766.summary: This inscription records a vow paid by Mullius and his associates, likely related to a successful hunt.

EDCS-08500769.translation: Tulla, daughter of Aucalon, willingly paid her vow to Minerva with a willing spirit.
EDCS-08500769.summary: This inscription records a vow paid by Tulla, daughter of Aucalon, to the goddess Minerva.

EDCS-08500770.translation: Optatus, son of Fronto, willingly and deservedly paid his vow to Minerva.
EDCS-08500770.summary: This inscription records a vow paid by Optatus, son of Fronto, to the goddess Minerva.

EDCS-08500771.translation: Attius Asper willingly and deservedly paid his vow to the Nymphs.
EDCS-08500771.summary: This inscription records a vow paid by Attius Asper to the Nymphs, deities associated with springs and nature.

EDCS-08500772.translation: Attis willingly and deservedly paid his vow to the Nymphs.
EDCS-08500772.summary: This inscription records a vow paid by Attis to the Nymphs, deities associated with springs and nature.

EDCS-08500773.translation: Caius Iuventius Honoratus, son of Marcus, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the Nymphs.
EDCS-08500773.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Caius Iuventius Honoratus to the Nymphs.

EDCS-08500775.translation: Lucius Bullonius Severus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Obio.
EDCS-08500775.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Lucius Bullonius Severus to Obio.

EDCS-08500776.translation: He/She gladly and deservedly offered a vow to the Parcae.
EDCS-08500776.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made to the Parcae.

EDCS-08500777.translation: Proxumis gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Canacia.
EDCS-08500777.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Proxumis to Canacia.

EDCS-08500778.translation: The slave of Aris Domitia Severina gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus, so that he might have Silvanus favorably disposed, as he wisely acted.
EDCS-08500778.summary: This inscription records a vow made by the slave of Aris Domitia Severina to Silvanus, asking for the god's favor.

EDCS-08500779.translation: Sextus Diuccius Primulus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus.
EDCS-08500779.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Sextus Diuccius Primulus to Silvanus.

EDCS-08500780.translation: Servatus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus.
EDCS-08500780.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Servatus to Silvanus.

EDCS-08500781.translation: Lucius Sescius Firmus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus.
EDCS-08500781.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Lucius Sescius Firmus to Silvanus.

EDCS-08500782.translation: Titus T M and Marcus A S gladly and deservedly fulfilled their vow to Silvanus.
EDCS-08500782.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Titus T M and Marcus A S to Silvanus.

EDCS-08500783.translation: Valerius Secundinus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus, by order.
EDCS-08500783.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Valerius Secundinus to Silvanus, given by order.

EDCS-08500784.summary: This dedication is made to the deities Silvanus and Silvana.

EDCS-08500785.translation: Marcus Vibius gladly fulfilled his vow to Vintur, as was deserved.
EDCS-08500785.summary: Marcus Vibius willingly paid a vow to Vintur, likely an individual or a deity, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500786.translation: Quintus Annius Bottus gladly fulfilled his vow to Uxovinus, as was deserved.
EDCS-08500786.summary: Quintus Annius Bottus willingly paid a vow to Uxovinus, likely a deity, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500787.translation: Gaius, son of A..., gladly fulfilled his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-08500787.summary: Gaius, son of A..., willingly paid a vow, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500788.summary: This inscription records an action taken by Aemilius under orders, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500790.translation: Helara, freedwoman of Caius, gladly fulfilled her vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-08500790.summary: Helara, a freedwoman of Caius, willingly paid a vow, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500791.translation: The associated traders, Titus Iulius Mopsinus and Valerius Nivalis, gladly fulfilled their vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-08500791.summary: Titus Iulius Mopsinus and Valerius Nivalis, identified as associated traders ('socii propoli'), willingly paid a vow together, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500792.translation: Qvae Vran Vic F Pri gladly fulfilled his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-08500792.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by an individual named Qvae Vran Vic F Pri, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500793.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Aurelius Euxenus, soldier, for his incomparable wife Quietia Avita, aged [---].
EDCS-08500793.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Aurelius Euxenus, a soldier, to his deceased wife Quietia Avita, noting her exceptional qualities and likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500794.translation: Titus Aemilius Gaius and Aemilius, as members of the college of the six, for themselves.
EDCS-08500794.summary: This inscription indicates that Titus Aemilius Gaius and Aemilius, who were members of a 'college of six' (VI viri), made this dedication for their own benefit or memory, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500795.translation: To Gaius Allius Celer, son of Gaius, of the Voltinia tribe, a four-man official, priest, augur of the colony Julia Apta, from the five decuriae, the villagers of Vordenses, to their patron.
EDCS-08500795.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the villagers of Vordenses to their patron, Gaius Allius Celer, who held several civic and religious offices in the colony of Julia Apta.

EDCS-08500796.translation: Gaius Allius Celer ordered this to be set up in his will to Lucius Allius Severus, his paternal uncle, and also two statues, for his father and himself; at the dedication of which statues, the heirs, according to the terms of the will, gave seventy denarii to each of the decurions.
EDCS-08500796.summary: This inscription records a testamentary dedication of a monument and statues by Gaius Allius Celer to his father and uncle, with a specific endowment for the decurions.

EDCS-08500797.translation: To Titus Camullius Aemilianus, son of Titus, of the Voltinia tribe, priest, four-man official of the colony Julia Agta; the council of Aptensium, satisfied with the honour, remitted the expenditure.
EDCS-08500797.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Titus Camullius Aemilianus, a four-man official and priest in the colony of Julia Agta, where the council waived costs due to his satisfaction with the honor.

EDCS-08500798.translation: To Julia Rogata, six-man official, Augustalis.
EDCS-08500798.summary: This is a short dedication to Julia Rogata, who held the positions of six-man official and Augustalis.

EDCS-08500799.translation: To the departed spirits of Orbia Maximilla, daughter of Titus, wife of Marcus, priestess Augusta of the colony Julia Apta; Vennonia Maximilla, daughter of Marcus, to her excellent mother, in accordance with her will.
EDCS-08500799.summary: This funerary dedication by Vennonia Maximilla to her mother, Orbia Maximilla, a priestess Augusta of the colony Julia Apta, was made according to the mother's will.

EDCS-08500800.translation: Valerius Fronto, four-man official, twice holder of the office of judge.
EDCS-08500800.summary: This inscription records the civic career of Valerius Fronto, who served twice as a four-man official and as a judge.

EDCS-08500801.translation: To the departed spirits of Lucius Volusius Severianus, son of Lucius, of the Voltinia tribe, four-man official of the colony Julia Apta, and priest, also two-man official of the colony Julia Hadriana Auenniensis, and pontifex, priest of the eternal city of Rome; Volusia Severiana, to her incomparable father.
EDCS-08500801.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Volusia Severiana to her father, Lucius Volusius Severianus, who held numerous high civic and religious offices in multiple colonies and in Rome.

EDCS-08500803.translation: Borysthenes the Alan Caesar's swift horse, who used to fly over sea and swamps and Etruscan hills and into the Pannonian plains, and no hunter dared to harm him with tooth, or he sprinkled the tip of his tail with saliva from his mouth as is usual; but he, untouched in youth and inviolate in limb, was struck down on his day; this is his burial place in the field.
EDCS-08500803.summary: This unusual inscription is a poetic epitaph for Borysthenes, a swift horse belonging to 'Caesar', detailing its prowess and peaceful death.

EDCS-08500804.summary: This inscription is a very fragmentary dedication, possibly to deities, by individuals named Alboinus and Berardus.

EDCS-08500805.summary: This is a simple dedication or statement of ownership by Aleasiumarae, daughter of Viri.

EDCS-08500806.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Camullia Terulla, a young woman.

EDCS-08500807.translation: Rufinus, a freedman, placed this at his own expense for Cornelia Servanda, daughter of Lucius.
EDCS-08500807.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by a freedman for Cornelia Servanda.

EDCS-08500808.translation: Fronto, son of Atepo, for himself and for his parents, in accordance with his will.
EDCS-08500808.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by Fronto for himself and his parents, as stipulated in his will.

EDCS-08500809.translation: To the Divine Shades. G. Severianus placed this for his most dutiful son, G. Lucilius, whom his freedman killed.
EDCS-08500809.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by G. Severianus for his son, G. Lucilius, who was murdered by his own freedman.

EDCS-08500810.translation: Titus Iulius Colonus made this for himself and for his wife, Secundina, daughter of Festo, and for his family, during his lifetime.
EDCS-08500810.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Titus Iulius Colonus for himself, his wife, and family.

EDCS-08500811.translation: Lucius Iulius Optatus, son of Lucius, of the Voltinia tribe, for himself and his family, [dedicated this] for Iulia Tertia, daughter of Sextus; Sextus Iulius, son of Sextus, of the Voltinia tribe; and Marcellina, daughter of Marcus, of the Voltinia tribe.
EDCS-08500811.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication by Lucius Iulius Optatus for himself, his family, and other relatives.

EDCS-08500812.translation: Quintus Iulius Iuventius for his dearest father, Quintus Iulius Victorinus.
EDCS-08500812.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by Quintus Iulius Iuventius for his father, Quintus Iulius Victorinus.

EDCS-08500813.translation: Caius Attius Novellus for his mother, Marcia Prisca, daughter of Quintus.
EDCS-08500813.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by Caius Attius Novellus for his mother, Marcia Prisca.

EDCS-08500814.translation: Novellia Paterna, daughter of Novellius, for herself, for her father Publius Novellius Novanus, for her mother Sabina Lenaei, for her brothers Publius Novellius Vasto and Lucius Novellius Sabinus, and for her nephews Quintus Veratius Vero and Sextus Veratius Paternus.
EDCS-08500814.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication by Novellia Paterna for herself, her parents, her brothers, and her nephews.

EDCS-08500815.translation: Onesimus for Onesimus, freedman of Quintus Licinius Quartus.
EDCS-08500815.summary: This is a funerary epitaph in which Onesimus dedicates a monument for Onesimus, the freedman of Quintus Licinius Quartus.

EDCS-08500816.translation: To Lucius Pompeius, son of Quintus.
EDCS-08500816.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Lucius Pompeius, son of Quintus.

EDCS-08500817.translation: To the Divine Manes, and to Libratia Marciana, an incomparable wife to Titus Pompeius Marcellus.
EDCS-08500817.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Libratia Marciana, wife of Titus Pompeius Marcellus.

EDCS-08500818.summary: This inscription records the name of Quintus Pompeius Montanus, likely on a tomb or dedication.

EDCS-08500819.summary: This inscription identifies Pompeia Rufina, daughter of Caius.

EDCS-08500820.translation: To Pompeia Trophime, his wife, from Titius Capito.
EDCS-08500820.summary: This is a dedication by Titius Capito to his wife, Pompeia Trophime.

EDCS-08500821.translation: ATI IO Porcius made (this) while alive.
EDCS-08500821.summary: This inscription records that Porcius made this monument during his lifetime.

EDCS-08500822.translation: A son placed (this) for his most dutiful father, Marcus Roscus Restutus.
EDCS-08500822.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by a son for his father, Marcus Roscus Restutus.

EDCS-08500823.translation: To the Divine Manes. Iulia Saturnina (placed this) for Satyrion. (He lived) 2 years.
EDCS-08500823.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Iulia Saturnina for Satyrion, indicating his age at death.

EDCS-08500826.translation: Titus Troccius Virilis ordered (this) to be made by his will for himself and for his wife, Maria Privata.
EDCS-08500826.summary: This inscription records that Titus Troccius Virilis willed this monument to be built for himself and his wife, Maria Privata.

EDCS-08500827.translation: To the Divine Manes. Valerius Iuventius (placed this) for his most dutiful son, Valerius Hilarius, whom the Fates above and below have touched; his absence pains me these years. He lived eight years, seven months, eight days.
EDCS-08500827.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Valerius Iuventius for his son Valerius Hilarius, who died at the age of eight years, seven months, and eight days.

EDCS-08500828.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Valeria, daughter of Iovigcorix.

EDCS-08500829.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Verbronara, daughter of Apetemar.

EDCS-08500834.translation: In front, 22 feet. Marcus R R.
EDCS-08500834.summary: This inscription, likely from a boundary stone or property marker, indicates a measurement of 22 feet and is attributed to Marcus.

EDCS-08500836.summary: This inscription lists several names, possibly of individuals associated with a Christian context.

EDCS-08500837.translation: Here rests Lector of good memory. He died in Christ, having departed on the 13th day before the Kalends of January.
EDCS-08500837.summary: This is a Christian funerary inscription for Lector, recording his death on December 20th.

EDCS-08500838.translation: Son of Mari Albarino willingly paid his vow deservedly.
EDCS-08500838.summary: This is a votive inscription by the son of Mari Albarino.

EDCS-08500840.translation: To the Genius of the colony. The IIIIvir Lucius Iulius Ianuarius Augustalis and Flavia dedicated this work to the IIIIvir[al college] with 4,000 sesterces of their own money.
EDCS-08500840.summary: This is a dedication by Lucius Iulius Ianuarius and Flavia to the Genius of the colony, funded by 4,000 sesterces for the IIIIvirate.

EDCS-08500841.translation: Aesilus, son of Savus, willingly paid his vow to Jupiter deservedly.
EDCS-08500841.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aesilus, son of Savus, to Jupiter.

EDCS-08500842.translation: Marcus Aquillius Avitus willingly paid his vow to Mars for his son Firminus, as deserved.
EDCS-08500842.summary: This is a votive inscription by Marcus Aquillius Avitus to Mars, made on behalf of his son Firminus.

EDCS-08500843.translation: Martialis willingly paid his vow to Mars for Silvester, as deserved.
EDCS-08500843.summary: This is a votive inscription by Martialis to Mars, made on behalf of Silvester.

EDCS-08500845.translation: To Mars, Quintus Sualeius Rufus gladly paid his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-08500845.summary: This dedication to Mars was made by Quintus Sualeius Rufus.

EDCS-08500846.translation: To Mars, Vinuleius, son of Vinicius, gladly paid his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-08500846.summary: This dedication to Mars was made by Vinuleius, son of Vinicius.

EDCS-08500848.translation: To Mars Augustus, Lucius Calvisius Aulinus gladly paid his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-08500848.summary: This dedication to Mars Augustus was made by Lucius Calvisius Aulinus.

EDCS-08500849.translation: To Mars, M. Firmus gladly paid his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-08500849.summary: This dedication to Mars was made by M. Firmus.

EDCS-08500850.translation: To Mars Nabelcus, Marcus Attius Sextinus gladly paid his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-08500850.summary: This dedication to Mars Nabelcus was made by Marcus Attius Sextinus.

EDCS-08500851.translation: To Mars Nabelcus, B. Marcianus gladly paid his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-08500851.summary: This dedication to Mars Nabelcus was made by B. Marcianus.

EDCS-08500852.translation: To Mars Nabelcus, Titus Iniush Severinus, on the advice of the deity, gladly paid his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-08500852.summary: This dedication to Mars Nabelcus was made by Titus Iniush Severinus, who was prompted by divine inspiration.

EDCS-08500853.translation: To Idonius, [someone] gladly paid [his] vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-08500853.summary: This dedication to a deity named Idonius records a vow being paid.

EDCS-08500855.translation: To the Elitinian Mothers, Capella Tessi gladly paid her vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-08500855.summary: This dedication to the Elitinian Mothers was made by Capella Tessi.

EDCS-08500856.translation: To Mercury, Aetius Masculus gladly paid his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-08500856.summary: This dedication to Mercury was made by Aetius Masculus.

EDCS-08500857.translation: Rufus, son of Sacconis, willingly and deservedly paid his vow to Mercury.
EDCS-08500857.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Mercury by Rufus, son of Sacconis.

EDCS-08500858.translation: Maxsumina, daughter of Maxsumus, willingly and deservedly paid her vow to the Nymphs.
EDCS-08500858.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the Nymphs by Maxsumina, daughter of Maxsumus.

EDCS-08500859.translation: Lucius Eppius Leo willingly and deservedly paid his vow to Uxsacanus.
EDCS-08500859.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Uxsacanus by Lucius Eppius Leo.

EDCS-08500860.translation: Valerius willingly paid his vow to Silvanus.
EDCS-08500860.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Silvanus by Valerius.

EDCS-08500861.translation: Tertius willingly and deservedly paid his vow to the Sulevis.
EDCS-08500861.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the Sulevis by Tertius.

EDCS-08500862.translation: Colia Maxuma [dedicated this] to the Sulevis of the Mountains.
EDCS-08500862.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the Sulevis Montanis by Colia Maxuma.

EDCS-08500864.translation: Lucius Vettius Verris and Titus Vettius Tetricus willingly and deservedly paid their vow.
EDCS-08500864.summary: This is a votive inscription made by Lucius Vettius Verris and Titus Vettius Tetricus.

EDCS-08500865.translation: Viurio Aius willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-08500865.summary: This is a votive inscription made by Viurio Aius.

EDCS-08500866.translation: Gaius Julius paid his vow to the Mountain Gods for himself and his family.
EDCS-08500866.summary: This is a votive inscription by Gaius Julius dedicated to the Mountain Gods for himself and his family.

EDCS-08500868.translation: To Marcus Hirtuleius Albanus, son of Marcus, soldier of the praetorian cohort.
EDCS-08500868.summary: This is an epitaph or dedication for Marcus Hirtuleius Albanus, a soldier of the praetorian cohort.

EDCS-08500869.translation: Gaius Veveius Fronto gave to the people a spring 30 feet long and 15 feet wide, and a road to the spring 4 feet wide, at his own expense.
EDCS-08500869.summary: This is a dedication of a public spring and road, funded by Gaius Veveius Fronto.

EDCS-08500870.translation: The college of association of carpenters of Apt, of Phoenices, made this at their own expense.
EDCS-08500870.summary: This records the construction of a building by a corporate association of carpenters from Apt.

EDCS-08500871.summary: An incomplete inscription identifying a person named Quintus Ari.

EDCS-08500873.translation: To Lucius Attius Successus, Lucilia Nice and her family, 20 feet in the field, 25 feet in the front.
EDCS-08500873.summary: This records a land grant or property marking for Lucius Attius Successus and Lucilia Nice, specifying dimensions.

EDCS-08500874.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aurelia Lucretia, daughter, placed this to her most dutiful father, dedicated under the axe.
EDCS-08500874.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Aurelia Lucretia to her father, Aurelius Euticus.

EDCS-08500875.translation: Antonatius and Flora, most unfortunate parents, made this for their sweetest and most dutiful son. Vinatius died on the 10th, rests, aged 22 years.
EDCS-08500875.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Vinatius, set up by his parents, recording his death and age.

EDCS-08500876.translation: To the Divine Shades. Lucius Valerius Paternus made this for Caecilia Secunda and for himself and his dearest wife.
EDCS-08500876.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Lucius Valerius Paternus for his wife Caecilia Secunda and himself.

EDCS-08500877.translation: To Caelius Pontius. Caelia [her daughter?] P. R. [while alive?]...
EDCS-08500877.summary: An incomplete funerary dedication to Caelius Pontius by Caelia, likely his daughter.

EDCS-08500878.translation: To the Divine Shades. Calliope placed this to her dearest husband, who lived 85 years.
EDCS-08500878.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Calliope to her husband, recording his age at death.

EDCS-08500879.translation: To the Divine Shades. Severina and Gaius Fur us [set this up for] their sweetest son, Lucius Calvisius Severus, who lived two years and five months.
EDCS-08500879.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Calvisius Severus, set up by his parents, recording his age at death.

EDCS-08500881.translation: To Lucius Coelius Vernon, 20 feet in front, 15 feet in the field. Primigenius, his freedman.
EDCS-08500881.summary: This is a boundary inscription marking land ownership, likely for Lucius Coelius Vernon, set up by his freedman Primigenius.

EDCS-08500882.translation: To Cornelius, son of Callo, for himself and his family, 15 feet in front, 20 feet in the field.
EDCS-08500882.summary: This boundary inscription records land dimensions and is for Cornelius and his family.

EDCS-08500883.translation: To Julia Paternus, Sestius Straton and Dexius Verus, with due regard.
EDCS-08500883.summary: This dedication, likely on a tomb, is for Julia Paternus by Sestius Straton and Dexius Verus.

EDCS-08500885.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Sextus Quartius for Martia Paullina, his freedwoman, M.A.I.I. [perhaps indicating years].
EDCS-08500885.summary: This funerary inscription is for Martia Paullina, a freedwoman of Sextus Quartius.

EDCS-08500886.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Sallustius Martialis set up this altar for his most dutiful mother, Sallustia Amabilis.
EDCS-08500886.summary: This is a funerary altar dedicated by Gaius Sallustius Martialis to his mother, Sallustia Amabilis.

EDCS-08500887.translation: To Secundinus, son of Marcus Antonius Lucius, the Third.
EDCS-08500887.summary: This inscription records the name of Secundinus, son of Marcus Antonius Lucius Tertius.

EDCS-08500888.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. [Soricina] and Maurilla made [this] for their dearest and most deserving husband and father Maurilio, who lived for 34 years.
EDCS-08500888.summary: This funerary inscription is for Maurilio, dedicated by his wife Soricina and daughter Maurilla, and records his age at death.

EDCS-08500889.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Lucius Tettius Cassianus. Pompeia Potentina [for] her most excellent husband.
EDCS-08500889.summary: This funerary inscription is for Lucius Tettius Cassianus, dedicated by his wife Pompeia Potentina.

EDCS-08500890.translation: Maltrebius, son of Publius Celer, arranged for the pleasure garden to be made at his own expense for Sextus Sentius, son of Sextus Sabatinus Primus, and his parents.
EDCS-08500890.summary: This inscription records the establishment of a pleasure garden funded by Maltrebius Celer for Sextus Sentius and his family.

EDCS-08500891.translation: To Valia Treptenus, his dearest wife, and for the burial place bought from Julius Actemus for one sestertius, Publius Decius [made this] for his foster-daughter, for her merits.
EDCS-08500891.summary: This funerary inscription, possibly recording a plot purchase, is dedicated by Publius Decius to his foster-daughter Valia Treptenus for her merits.

EDCS-08500892.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, for Veratius, son of Veratius, a most devoted husband to Valeria.
EDCS-08500892.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to Veratius by his wife Valeria, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-08500894.translation: Here rests Bohetyus of good memory, bishop, who lived as bishop for 20 years and 6 months, died on the 10th day before the Kalends of June, in the seventh indiction.
EDCS-08500894.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Bishop Bohetyus, dating his death to the 7th indiction (likely 6th century CE given the mention of indictions and episcopal office).

EDCS-08500899.translation: Titus Titius Felicio willingly and deservedly paid his vow to Jupiter.
EDCS-08500899.summary: This is a votive inscription by Titus Titius Felicio dedicating a vow to Jupiter, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-08500901.translation: He willingly and deservedly paid his vow to Mars.
EDCS-08500901.summary: This is a votive inscription, likely from the Roman imperial period, dedicating a vow to Mars.

EDCS-08500902.translation: Decimus Vinuleius Atticus willingly and deservedly paid his vow to Mars.
EDCS-08500902.summary: This is a votive inscription by Decimus Vinuleius Atticus dedicating a vow to Mars, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-08500906.translation: Paternus, son of Certulus, willingly and deservedly paid his vow to Silvanus Augustus.
EDCS-08500906.summary: This is a votive inscription by Paternus, son of Certulus, dedicating a vow to Silvanus Augustus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-08500908.translation: Publius Apicius Modestus, with money from a fine.
EDCS-08500908.summary: This inscription records Publius Apicius Modestus using funds obtained from a legal penalty, possibly for a public or religious dedication, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-08500909.summary: This inscription likely refers to Licinia Tertia acting on behalf of or dedicating something for Prima Tax, the meaning of TAX is unclear, probably from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-08500910.translation: Naevius Valentinus and Naevius Lucanus willingly and deservedly paid their vow.
EDCS-08500910.summary: This is a votive inscription by Naevius Valentinus and Naevius Lucanus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-08500912.translation: Vidillus, son of Avot, from Dacurdus, from Mario Sacroviros, (from) Ostre, Catus, son of Boducacus, from Vaune, Bene, (and) Avot, spoke.
EDCS-08500912.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or record made by several individuals, possibly of local or tribal origin, with uncertain context and dating, but likely from a Roman provincial setting.
